Finance Analyst Toolkit
Step 1 of 3 · Your Goal
What are you preparing for?
We'll customise your study plan based on your target role.
🔬
Equity Research Analyst
Fundamental analysis, valuation, stock pitching
🏦
Investment Banking Analyst
M&A, LBO, DCF, financial modelling
💼
PE / VC Associate
Deal sourcing, due diligence, returns analysis
📈
General Finance Role
FP&A, corporate finance, accounting
Step 2 of 3 · Daily Goal
How many cards per day?
Consistency beats intensity. Even 10 cards daily compounds massively over weeks.
10
Casual
20
Focused
30
Intensive
Step 3 of 3 · Focus Areas
Which topics to prioritise?
Select all that apply. We'll weight your study sessions accordingly.

Revenue Models by Industry
Deep reference · Indian comps · Modelling nuances · Select a sector to explore
🏪
Retail
SSSG · Store count · Private label · Ind AS 116
Bottom-up
🏦
Banking / NBFC
NIM · Loan growth · NPA · Credit cost
Bottom-up
💻
IT Services
HC × Utilisation × Billing rate · TCV · CC growth
Bottom-up
🛒
FMCG
Volume × Price × Mix · Rural demand · A&P
Bottom-up
💊
Pharma
IPM · US generics · ANDA pipeline · FDA risk
Bottom-up
📡
Telecom
Subs × ARPU · Tariff hikes · Home BB / FWA
Bottom-up
🏗️
Real Estate
Presales · Collections · Ind AS 115 · NAV
Bottom-up
🚗
Auto / Manufacturing
Volume × ASP · Steel costs · EV mix · SIAM
Bottom-up
⚡
Power / Infra
Capacity × PLF × Tariff · PPA · DISCOM risk
Bottom-up
🌐
Internet / Tech
GMV × Take rate · CAC/LTV · Contribution margin
Top-down
← All Sectors
Sectors
🏪 Retail
🏦 Banking / NBFC
💻 IT Services
🛒 FMCG
💊 Pharma
📡 Telecom

🌐 Internet / Tech
🏪
Retail
Revenue = Store count × Revenue per store. SSSG and new store ramp are the two core levers. Never apply SSSG to stores open less than 12 months.
Bottom-up
Revenue Streams
Product salesPrivate labelFranchise feesAnchor tenant rentalOnline / omnichannel
Core KPIs
SSSG %Revenue / sq ftGross margin %Inventory daysStore count CAGREBITDA / store
Key Drivers
Store count
Existing + planned openings. Split by format. Track net adds quarterly.
SSSG %
Same-store sales growth — most watched retail metric. Only stores open ≥12 months.
New store ramp
~50% Y1 → 80% Y2 → 100% Y3 productivity. Never apply SSSG.
Avg ticket × footfall
Footfall × conversion % × ASP × items per bill.
Forecast Formula
Revenue = Mature stores × RevPerStore × (1+SSSG)
+ New stores × MatureRevPerStore × Ramp%
Private label: 200–500bps higher GM vs branded
DMart benchmark: ₹35,000+/sq ft
Indian Comps (FY24)
Company Format Stores Rev/Store Gross Margin SSSG
DMart Value hypermarket ~365 stores ₹100–120 Cr/store ~15% GM +7–9% SSSG
Trent (Tata) Westside + Zudio ~800+ ₹4–6 Cr blended ~47% +22%+
Shoppers Stop Premium dept. ~105 ₹25–30 Cr ~40% +5–7%
Common Pitfalls
⚠
Applying SSSG to new stores — only valid for stores open ≥12 months
⚠
Ignoring Ind AS 116 lease impact on EBITDA — looks 400–800bps inflated
⚠
Headline revenue ≠ net revenue — reconcile to net revenue from operations
Analyst Tips
💡
Revenue/sq ft is the primary productivity metric — normalises across store sizes
💡
Decompose like-for-like vs new store contribution — SSSG growth valued higher by market
💡
Tier 2/3 expansion is the current growth frontier — Zudio, V2 Retail
🏦
Banking / NBFC
Revenue = NII + Fee + Treasury. Loan book compounds; NIM is the rate of return on that book. Credit cost is what takes it all away.
Bottom-up
Revenue Streams
Net Interest Income (NII)Fee & commissionTreasury / trading gainsInsurance distributionForex / remittance
Core KPIs
NIM %Loan growth %GNPA %ROA / ROEPCR %Cost-to-incomeCASA ratio
Key Drivers
Loan book (AUM)
Opening book × (1+loan growth%). Model segment-wise: retail/SME/corporate/agri.
NIM
Yield on advances – Cost of funds. Compresses in rate-cut cycles.
Credit cost
Provisions / Avg loan book. Swings PAT by 20–30% in stress cycles.
Fee income
Fee as % of avg assets. Fee-heavy banks more resilient across rate cycles.
Forecast Formula
NII = Avg Loan Book × NIM
Net Revenue = NII + Fee + Treasury
PPOP = Net Revenue – Opex
PAT = PPOP – Provisions – Tax
NIM = Yield on advances – Cost of funds
Indian Comps (FY24)
Company Type Loan Book NIM % ROA % GNPA %
HDFC Bank Private bank ~₹25L Cr 3.4–3.6% NIM ROA 1.9% GNPA 1.3%
SBI PSU bank ~₹37L Cr 3.2–3.4% ROA 1.0% GNPA 2.2%
Bajaj Finance NBFC ~₹3.5L Cr 9.5–10% ROA 4.5% GNPA 0.9%
Common Pitfalls
⚠
Confusing NII with total net revenue — add fee + treasury for full picture
⚠
NIM compression lag in rate-cut cycles — model asymmetric response
⚠
NBFC vs Bank NIM not comparable — NBFCs have no CASA, higher CoF
Analyst Tips
💡
Build DuPont tree: ROE = ROA × Leverage — separates asset quality from structure
💡
PPOP is the cleanest normalised profit metric — removes credit cost volatility
💡
GNPA context matters — Shriram 5.3% reflects used-CV/SME segment, not mortgage quality
💻
IT Services
Revenue = Billable HC × Utilisation × Billing rate. Deal wins and client mining drive long-term compounding. Always use Constant Currency for demand comparison.
Bottom-up (HC-based)
Revenue Streams
Time & Material (T&M)Fixed-price contractsManaged services / AMSProducts / IP licensingBPO / KPO
Core KPIs
Revenue / employee ($)Utilisation %Attrition %Deal TCV ($)EBIT margin %CC revenue growth %
Key Drivers
Headcount (billable)
Net hiring = gross additions – attrition. Billable %: ~78–83% for large IT cos.
Utilisation rate
Billable hrs / available hrs. Healthy: 80–85%. Below 78% = bench cost pressure.
Billing rate (ARPE)
Avg revenue per billable employee. US $48–65K/yr for Indian IT cos.
Deal TCV pipeline
Total contract value — converts to revenue over 3–5 years avg.
Forecast Formula
Revenue = Billable HC × Utilisation% × Billing rate
INR Revenue = USD Revenue × Avg USD/INR
₹1 INR depreciation → ~50–70bps EBIT tailwind
Large deal TCV ramp: revenue over 12–18 months
Indian Comps (FY24)
Company Revenue Headcount EBIT Margin Attrition Rev/Emp
TCS ~$29B 6,01,000 EBIT 24–26% Attrition 12.5% $48K/emp
Infosys ~$18.6B 3,15,000 20–21% 12.7% $59K
HCL Tech ~$13.3B 2,27,000 18–19% 13.0% $59K
Common Pitfalls
⚠
Using INR revenue without CC adjustment — use Constant Currency for demand comparison
⚠
Deal TCV ≠ near-term revenue — large deals ramp over 12–24 months
⚠
Ignoring utilisation lag during aggressive hiring — 2–3 quarter lag before revenue benefit
Analyst Tips
💡
Track revenue/employee as productivity trend — HC growing faster than revenue = warning signal
💡
Deal TCV commentary leads consensus by 2 quarters — early tracking gives earnings edge
💡
Model GenAI as both headwind (billing compression) and tailwind (deal scope expansion)
🛒
FMCG
Volume × price is the core equation. Rural demand cycles, commodity costs, and distribution make FMCG modelling deceptively nuanced. Always decompose VPM.
Bottom-up (Volume × Price)
Revenue Streams
Domestic branded salesExport revenueModern trade / e-commerceB2B / institutional
Core KPIs
Volume growth %Price / mix growthGross margin %A&P % of salesDistribution reachRural vs Urban split
Key Drivers
Volume growth
Category market growth × company share change. Nielsen / Kantar for data.
Pricing / mix
Price hikes (pass-through) + premiumisation (mix shift to higher-price SKUs).
Distribution reach
Direct reach in towns — numeric vs weighted distribution is the granular measure.
Raw material cost
Palm oil, wheat, sugar, crude (packaging). Track commodity futures monthly.
Forecast Formula
Revenue = Volume × Realisation per unit
Revenue growth = Volume% + Price% + Mix%
Gross Margin = (Revenue – RM cost) / Revenue
A&P: 10–13% of sales — do not cut (protects moat)
Indian Comps (FY24)
Company Revenue Gross Margin EBITDA Margin Volume Growth Rural %
HUL ~₹59,000 Cr GM 50–52% EBITDA 23–24% Vol +2–4% Rural 45–50%
Nestle India ~₹18,000 Cr 55–57% 22–24% +6–8% ~30%
Dabur India ~₹13,000 Cr 47–49% 19–21% +5–7% ~50%
Common Pitfalls
⚠
Headline revenue masking volume decline — decompose VPM, volume is the real demand signal
⚠
Treating A&P as cuttable cost — erodes brand equity and market share structurally
⚠
Ignoring channel mix shift — e-commerce has lower net realisations for FMCG cos
Analyst Tips
💡
Track Nielsen/Kantar every quarter — market share shifts matter more than volume in short run
💡
Rural recovery is the macro call — good monsoon → kharif → rural income → FMCG (1–2Q lag)
💡
Premiumisation is structural — secular shift to premium SKUs is a 5–10yr margin tailwind
💊
Pharma
Revenue = India + US + EM + API/CDMO. Segment-wise modelling is non-negotiable — US generics dynamics are completely different from India branded business.
Bottom-up (segment-wise)
Revenue Streams
India formulations (IPM)US genericsEmerging markets (EM)API / CDMOBranded generics (EU/ROW)
Core KPIs
ANDA filings / approvalsUS revenue %IPM growth vs marketR&D % of salesEBITDA margin %
Key Drivers
India (IPM)
IPM market growth × company share. IQVIA reports IPM monthly.
US generics
ANDA approvals × launch price minus structural price erosion (~8% p.a. base).
Price erosion (US)
Structural 5–15% YoY pricing pressure unless Para IV exclusivity.
New launches
Pending ANDAs × launch probability × peak revenue per product.
Forecast Formula
Revenue = India + US + EM + API/CDMO
India: IPM growth% × company market share
US: (Base – erosion%) + new launches
Para IV: 180-day exclusivity → outsized Y1 revenue
+₹1 depreciation → +1–2% revenue, +50bps EBITDA
Indian Comps (FY24)
Company Revenue US Rev% India Rev% EBITDA Margin ANDA Pipeline
Sun Pharma ~₹48,000 Cr US 32% India 33% EBITDA 28–30% 700+ filings
Dr. Reddys ~₹28,000 Cr US 45% India 18% 23–25% ~100 pending
Cipla ~₹25,000 Cr US 25% India 40% 22–24% 300+ filings
Common Pitfalls
⚠
Ignoring US price erosion — ~8% annual structural erosion overstates revenue by 20–30% over 3 yrs
⚠
FDA warning letter = revenue cliff — single action can wipe 15–25% of US revenue instantly
⚠
Confusing cumulative ANDA filings with pending approvals — pending pipeline is what matters
Analyst Tips
💡
Build product-level US model — top 5 products = 30–50% of US revenue
💡
Specialty pharma commands premium multiples — specialty % of US revenue is the re-rating driver
💡
CDMO/API = higher-quality revenue — long-term contracts, better visibility, higher margins
📡
Telecom
Revenue = Subs × ARPU. India is a functional duopoly (Jio + Airtel). ARPU trajectory through periodic tariff hikes is the single most important assumption.
Bottom-up (Subs × ARPU)
Revenue Streams
Mobile prepaidMobile postpaidHome broadband / FWAEnterprise / B2BTower rental
Core KPIs
ARPU ₹/monthSubscriber net addsChurn %Market share %EBITDA margin %Net debt/EBITDA
Key Drivers
Subscriber base
Track VLR (active on network) not HLR (registered SIMs).
ARPU (₹/month)
Single most important lever. Airtel FY24: ~₹208. Jio: ~₹182.
Tariff hike timing
Typical cadence: 1 hike every 18–24 months. Model pro-rata quarterly impact.
Home broadband (FWA)
5G Fixed Wireless Access — ARPU ~₹500–700/mo vs mobile ~₹200.
Forecast Formula
Mobile Rev = Mobile subs × ARPU × 12
Home BB Rev = Home BB subs × BB ARPU × 12
Total = Mobile + Home BB + Enterprise
EBITDA margin: ~52–55% for Jio and Airtel
Model tariff hike as pro-rata ARPU step-up
Indian Comps (FY24)
Company Revenue ARPU (₹/mo) Subscribers EBITDA Margin Status
Reliance Jio ~₹1,07,000 Cr ₹182 ARPU ~490 Mn subs EBITDA 52–54%
Bharti Airtel ~₹1,50,000 Cr ₹208 ~370 Mn 53–55%
Vodafone Idea ~₹44,000 Cr ₹146 ~215 Mn 40–43% Distressed
Common Pitfalls
⚠
Using HLR subscribers for ARPU — use VLR (active/on-network) only
⚠
Strong EBITDA ≠ strong FCF — 5G capex ₹40,000–50,000 Cr/yr eats up FCF
⚠
Modelling tariff hike as full-quarter ARPU jump — pro-rate if mid-quarter
Analyst Tips
💡
India ARPU ~$2.5/month vs global ~$10+ — partial catch-up is a multi-year re-rating story
💡
Home broadband is the next subscriber wave — ~35 Mn subs vs 490 Mn+ mobile data users
💡
Vi decline is structural tailwind for duopoly — churners go to Jio/Airtel at higher ARPU
🏗️
Real Estate
Presales ≠ P&L revenue. Under Ind AS 115, revenue recognised on % completion — not at booking. This is the #1 modelling trap in real estate.
Bottom-up (Units × Price)
Revenue Streams
Residential presalesCommercial leasingPlotted developmentHospitality / retail assets
Core KPIs
Presales value (₹ Cr)Collections (₹ Cr)Realization / sq ftNet debtLaunch pipeline GDV
Key Drivers
Presales (bookings)
Units sold × avg realization. Track area sold and value separately.
Collections
Construction-linked cash inflows. Lags presales by 2–3 years.
P&L recognition
Ind AS 115: % completion method. P&L ≠ presales in same year.
Launch pipeline / GDV
GDV of planned launches drives presales 2–4 quarters forward.
Forecast Formula
Presales = Area sold (sq ft) × Avg realization (₹/sq ft)
Collections = f(presales of prior 2–3 years)
P&L Revenue = % completion × Project GDV (Ind AS 115)

Presales = demand | Collections = cash | P&L = accounting
Rental: Area × Occupancy% × Rent (₹/sq ft/mo) × 12
Indian Comps (FY24)
Company Presales Collections GDV Pipeline Focus
DLF ₹14,778 Cr presales ₹12,000+ Cr collections GDV ₹91,000 Cr+ Luxury + Rental
Godrej Properties ₹22,527 Cr ₹12,000+ Cr ₹5L Cr+ pipeline Pan-India
Prestige Estates ₹21,040 Cr ₹12,000+ Cr ₹70,000 Cr South India
Common Pitfalls
⚠
Using presales as current-year P&L revenue — flows into P&L over 2–4 years
⚠
Ignoring project-level EBITDA variability — lumpy luxury handovers inflate reported margins
⚠
DLF "net cash" = dev co only — DCCDL rental sub carries its own debt
Analyst Tips
💡
NAV is the primary valuation anchor — P/NAV = market cap ÷ sum of project NPVs
💡
Collections > Presales = balance sheet strength — converting demand to cash
💡
Quarterly presales gives 6–12 months forward visibility on P&L revenue
🚗
Auto / Mfg
Revenue = Volume × ASP across segments (2W, PV, CV, EV). Cyclicality is the defining feature — volumes track GDP, credit availability, commodity costs, and fuel prices.
Bottom-up (Volume × ASP)
Revenue Streams
Vehicle sales (domestic)ExportsSpare parts / aftermarketCaptive financial servicesEV / mobility
Core KPIs
Volume (units) CAGRAvg selling price (ASP)EBITDA margin %Market share %EV % of mixDealer inventory days
Key Drivers
Domestic volume
Industry volume (SIAM) × company market share. Each segment moves differently.
Avg selling price (ASP)
Mix shift (premium models) + price hikes vs commodity pass-through.
Input costs
Steel (~30% COGS), aluminium, semiconductors, rubber. Track LME monthly.
EV mix
ASP-accretive but margin-dilutive today; as battery costs fall margins converge.
Forecast Formula
Revenue = Σ (Segment volume × Segment ASP)
Segment margins: 2W ~14–17% | PV ~8–12% | CV ~10–14%
Steel sensitivity: ₹1,000/MT change → ~20–40bps EBITDA
EV: ASP ₹2–5L higher vs ICE but EBITDA 200–400bps lower
Indian Comps (FY24)
Company Segment Revenue Volume EBITDA Margin
Maruti Suzuki PV dominant ~40% share ~₹1,41,000 Cr 21.8L units EBITDA 12.1%
M&M (standalone) UV + Farm + EV ~₹1,22,000 Cr 9.6L units 9–10%
Bajaj Auto 2W + 3W + exports ~₹46,000 Cr ~40L+ units 20–21%
Common Pitfalls
⚠
Ignoring segment cyclicality — CV 2× more cyclical than PV; blend masks this
⚠
Wholesale ≠ retail sellthrough — dealer inventory buildup signals demand stress 1Q early
⚠
Tata Motors blended EBITDA is JLR-dominated — analyse JLR and India separately
Analyst Tips
💡
SIAM monthly data is a pre-earnings leading indicator — update model before consensus
💡
EV is ASP-accretive but margin-dilutive now — as battery costs fall (2026+) margins converge
💡
VAHAN portal gives real retail sellthrough — cross-check against OEM wholesale dispatches
⚡
Power / Infra
Revenue = electricity generated × tariff. Regulated utilities are bond-like. Renewable players have high earnings visibility through long-term PPAs.
Bottom-up (Capacity × PLF × Tariff)
Revenue Streams
Thermal generation (PPA)Merchant power (IEX)Solar / Wind (PPA)Transmission chargesDISCOM distribution
Core KPIs
PLF % (thermal)CUF % (solar/wind)Tariff (₹/kWh)PPA coverage %Capacity MW (operational)Net debt/EBITDA
Key Drivers
Installed capacity (MW)
Only operational capacity generates revenue — not pipeline or under-construction.
PLF / CUF
Thermal PLF: 65–80%. Solar CUF: 22–28%. Wind CUF: 28–38%.
Tariff (₹/kWh)
PPA: fixed ~₹2.5–4/kWh. Merchant: IEX spot ~₹4–8/kWh (volatile).
New capacity
Under-construction × commissioning timeline. Typical lag: 18–36 months from FID.
Forecast Formula
Units (MU) = Capacity (MW) × PLF% × 8,760 hrs
Revenue = MU generated × Tariff (₹/kWh)
Thermal: Capacity charge + Energy charge
Renewable: Energy charge only (no capacity charge)
PPA → fixed tariff 25-yr | Merchant → volatile, margin-rich
Indian Comps (FY24)
Company Type Capacity PLF / CUF Revenue EBITDA Margin
NTPC Thermal + RE ~73 GW PLF 73–75% ~₹1,75,000 Cr EBITDA 30–32%
Adani Green Solar + Wind ~10.9 GW CUF ~27% ~₹9,000 Cr 87–90%
Power Grid Corp Transmission ~1,75,000 CKT km N/A ~₹46,000 Cr 85–87%
Common Pitfalls
⚠
Confusing PLF (thermal) with CUF (renewable) — Solar CUF 25% is excellent; ≠ thermal PLF 75%
⚠
Counting pipeline/under-construction as revenue — only operational MW generates revenue
⚠
Ignoring DISCOM counterparty risk — state DISCOMs frequently delay PPA payments 12–24 months
Analyst Tips
💡
Regulated businesses: use P/BV × allowed RoE (CERC allows ~15.5% RoE on regulated equity)
💡
India 500GW RE target: 30–35 GW/year additions needed through 2030 — multi-year order book visibility
💡
Renewable EBITDA 87–90% makes sense — PPA revenue, near-zero variable cost; real risk is D&A + interest
🌐
Internet / Tech
Revenue from GMV take rate, subscriptions, ads, or SaaS. The only sector where top-down is preferred — P&L may be negative for years.
Top-down (TAM × Penetration)
Revenue Streams
GMV take rate (marketplace)Subscription / SaaSAdvertising (platform)Fintech / embedded financeCloud / platform fees
Core KPIs
GMV growth %Take rate %Contribution marginCAC / LTV ratioMAU / DAUPath to EBITDA breakeven
Key Drivers
GMV growth
Transacting users × order frequency × avg order value (AOV).
Take rate %
Revenue ÷ GMV. Zomato: ~22–23%. Never confuse with inventory model net margin.
MAU / DAU
DAU/MAU stickiness ratio indicates product quality and monetisation potential.
Contribution margin
Revenue – variable costs. First proof of unit economics. Must turn positive first.
Forecast Formula
Revenue = GMV × Take rate [pure marketplace]
Revenue = Subscribers × ARPU [SaaS / subscription]
GMV = Transacting users × Orders/user × AOV
Sequence: Contribution margin +ve → EBITDA +ve → PAT +ve
Indian Comps (FY24)
Company Business Model Scale Margin Metric EBITDA Status
Zomato Food + Blinkit GOV ~₹72,000 Cr; take rate ~22% Contribution margin 5–6% EBITDA +ve FY24
Nykaa Beauty e-comm (hybrid) GMV ~₹7,800 Cr; net rev/GMV ~35% ~6–7% contribution EBITDA +ve
Delhivery B2B + B2C logistics Revenue ~₹8,000 Cr ~5–6% contribution Near breakeven
Common Pitfalls
⚠
Valuing on GMV EV multiples — GMV is gross volume; always convert to revenue multiples
⚠
Conflating Nykaa net revenue/GMV with a take rate — it is an inventory-carrying model
⚠
Ignoring ESOP cost in "adjusted EBITDA" — can be 5–15% of revenue for growth-stage companies
Analyst Tips
💡
Contribution margin +ve is the first real milestone — earliest proof of business model viability
💡
Blinkit is Zomato hidden value driver — analysts modelling only food delivery undervalue Zomato
💡
Embedded fintech = highest-margin revenue layer — near-zero marginal cost on top of existing platform

Statements

Cash Flow

Turnover Ratios
Asset turnover · DSO · DIO · DPO · CCC · WC efficiency
Ratios

Liquidity Ratios
Current · Quick · Cash ratio · Operating CF ratio · NWC
Ratios
🏛️
Solvency Ratios
D/E · Net Debt/EBITDA · ICR · DSCR · Promoter pledge
Ratios

Profitability Ratios
Margins · ROE · ROCE · ROA · ROIC · DuPont
Ratios

P/E · EV/EBITDA · P/B · PEG · Dividend yield · EV/Capacity
Ratios
← All Topics
Statements
📊 Income Statement
🏦 Balance Sheet
💵 Cash Flow
Ratios
⚙️ Turnover
💧 Liquidity
🏛️ Solvency
💰 Profitability
📈 Valuation
The Income Statement (P&L) shows a company's financial performance over a period — revenue earned, costs incurred, and profit generated. It runs on accrual accounting — revenue is recognised when earned, not when cash is received.
Standard P&L Structure
(+) Other Income / (Loss)
Revenue (Net Sales) Gross sales minus returns, discounts, rebates
(-) Cost of Goods Sold (COGS) Direct material + labour + manufacturing overhead
= Gross Profit Revenue − COGS
(-) SG&A Expenses Sales, general & administrative costs
(-) R&D Expenses Research & development spend (pharma/tech)
(-) Depreciation & Amortisation Non-cash charge for asset usage over time
= EBIT (Operating Income) Gross Profit − Opex − D&A
(-) Interest Expense Cost of debt; tax-deductible
Treasury gains, forex, dividend from subs
= PBT (Pre-Tax Income) EBIT − Interest + Other Income
(-) Income Tax (Current + Deferred) Tax at applicable rate; DTL/DTA adjustments
= PAT (Net Income) Profit after all costs and taxes
(-) Minority Interest Profit attributable to non-controlling stake
= Attributable PAT Profit belonging to parent company shareholders
Key Subtotals — What Analysts Use
EBITDA EBIT + D&A. Proxy for operating cash flow; used in EV/EBITDA, Net Debt/EBITDA
EBIT Earnings before interest & tax. Capital-structure neutral operating profit
EBT / PBT Earnings before tax. EBIT after financing costs
PAT Net income. Drives EPS, ROE, P/E multiple
Adjusted PAT PAT stripped of one-time items for recurring earnings view
EPS (Basic) PAT ÷ Weighted avg basic shares outstanding
EPS (Diluted) PAT ÷ Fully diluted shares (incl. ESOPs, warrants)
India-Specific P&L Line Items
Exceptional Items One-off gains/losses (impairment, VRS, restructuring) — shown separately above tax line
Deferred Tax Non-cash tax arising from timing differences (accelerated depreciation, provisions)
MAT Credit Minimum Alternate Tax credit — set off against future normal tax liability
Share of Profit (Associates) Equity method earnings from associates (20–49% ownership)
Other Comprehensive Income (OCI) Mark-to-market on investments, actuarial gains — bypasses P&L per Ind AS
Key Formulas
Gross Profit= Revenue − COGS Gross Margin %= Gross Profit ÷ Revenue × 100 EBITDA= Revenue − COGS − Opex + D&A EBIT= EBITDA − D&A PBT= EBIT − Interest + Other Income PAT= PBT × (1 − Effective Tax Rate) EPS = PAT ÷ Weighted Avg Diluted SharesDuPont: ROE = PAT Margin × Asset Turnover × Equity Multiplier
Margin Benchmarks — India Listed Cos
Gross Margin FMCG: 48–58% | IT: 30–35% | Pharma: 60–68% | Retail: 14–18%
EBITDA Margin FMCG: 22–28% | IT: 20–26% | Telecom: 48–55% | Auto OEM: 10–14%
EBIT Margin IT services: 18–24% | Pharma: 18–26% | Banking: use NIM/ROA
PAT Margin FMCG: 14–18% | IT: 14–20% | Retail (DMart): 5–6%
Tax Rate (India) New regime: 25.17% | Old regime: 34.94% | MAT: 15%
Common Pitfalls & Analyst Tips
⚠
Treating EBITDA as cash flowEBITDA excludes capex, working capital changes, and interest. A company with ₹1,000 Cr EBITDA but ₹800 Cr capex has very little free cash. Always check FCF = EBITDA − CapEx − ΔNWC − Interest − Tax.
⚠
Ignoring Other Income in PATMany Indian companies report strong PAT growth driven by treasury income, asset sales, or forex gains — not operations. Strip other income to assess core business PAT margin trend.
⚠
Missing Ind AS 116 EBITDA inflationPost Ind AS 116, lease payments move below EBITDA (to D&A + Interest). Retail and airline EBITDA margins look 400–800bps higher than pre-Ind AS 116. Always compare on like-for-like basis.
💡
Build a clean/adjusted P&L alongside reportedStrip exceptional items, one-time gains, and Other Income to arrive at recurring EBITDA and recurring PAT. This is what institutional investors value on, not reported numbers.
💡
Track operating leverage carefullyA business with high fixed costs (telecom, airlines) has significant operating leverage — small revenue increase → large EBITDA increase. Model contribution margin separately from fixed costs for accuracy.
💡
Revenue ≠ Cash collectedUnder accrual accounting, revenue is recognised at point of delivery/service, not cash receipt. Always reconcile revenue growth with A/R and collection efficiency to check for channel stuffing.
The Balance Sheet is a snapshot of what a company owns (Assets) and how it is financed (Liabilities + Equity) at a point in time. The fundamental equation: Assets = Liabilities + Shareholders' Equity — must always balance.
Assets Side
NON-CURRENT ASSETS
Property, Plant & Equipment (PP&E) Tangible fixed assets net of accumulated depreciation
Capital Work-in-Progress (CWIP) Assets under construction — not yet depreciated
Intangible Assets Patents, trademarks, software, goodwill
Right-of-Use (ROU) Assets Lease assets under Ind AS 116 — capitalised leases
Investments (Long-term) Equity/debt investments in subsidiaries, associates
Deferred Tax Asset (DTA) Future tax benefit from timing differences
CURRENT ASSETS
Inventories Raw materials, WIP, finished goods at lower of cost/NRV
Trade Receivables (A/R) Amounts owed by customers for goods/services delivered
Cash & Cash Equivalents Cash + short-term liquid investments (<3 months)
Short-term Investments Mutual funds, T-bills, FDs maturing within 12 months
Loans & Advances (ST) Prepayments, advance to vendors, security deposits
= Total Assets Must equal Total Liabilities + Equity
Liabilities & Equity Side
SHAREHOLDERS' EQUITY
Share Capital Paid-up capital (face value × shares outstanding)
Securities Premium Amount raised above face value in equity issuances
Retained Earnings Cumulative PAT not distributed as dividends
Other Reserves & Surplus Capital reserves, revaluation reserves, OCI
Minority Interest (NCI) Equity of non-controlling shareholders in subsidiaries
NON-CURRENT LIABILITIES
Long-term Borrowings Bonds, term loans, debentures due >1 year
Lease Liabilities (LT) Long-term portion of Ind AS 116 lease obligations
Deferred Tax Liability (DTL) Future tax obligation from timing differences
Provisions (LT) Gratuity, pension, warranty obligations >1 year
CURRENT LIABILITIES
Trade Payables (A/P) Amounts owed to suppliers for goods/services received
Short-term Borrowings Working capital loans, commercial paper, bank OD
Current Maturities of LT Debt Portion of long-term debt due within 12 months
Deferred Revenue Cash received but services not yet performed
Accrued Expenses Expenses incurred but not yet paid (wages, utilities)
= Total Liabilities + Equity Must equal Total Assets
Key Formulas & Relationships
Assets= Liabilities + Shareholders' Equity Shareholders' Equity= Total Assets − Total Liabilities Net Debt= Total Debt − Cash − Short-term Investments Capital Employed= Equity + Debt (or Total Assets − Current Liabilities) Working Capital= Current Assets − Current Liabilities Book Value/Share= Shareholders' Equity ÷ Shares Outstanding Enterprise Value = Market Cap + Net Debt + Minority InterestTangible Book Value = Equity − Goodwill − Intangibles
Balance Sheet Quality Checks
Goodwill / Total Assets High goodwill = acquisition-driven growth; watch for impairment risk
CWIP / Gross Block High CWIP = capex cycle; revenue benefit comes post-commissioning
A/R / Revenue (days) Rising DSO = collection risk or aggressive revenue recognition
Promoter Pledge % India-specific: pledged shares >30% = significant risk signal
Contingent Liabilities Shown in notes — can be large (tax disputes, legal claims)
Related-party payables Large RPT balances = governance concern; check notes carefully
Common Pitfalls & Analyst Tips
⚠
Book value ≠ Market valueBook value is based on historical cost less accumulated depreciation. Market value reflects future earnings potential. For asset-light businesses (IT, FMCG), book value is almost meaningless — market values intangibles like brand, distribution, and talent that don't appear on the balance sheet.
⚠
Missing contingent liabilities in notesLarge tax disputes, guarantees, and litigation are disclosed only in the notes to accounts — not on the face of the balance sheet. These can be material (especially for Indian companies facing GST, income tax, or customs disputes).
⚠
Ind AS 116: ROU assets inflate balance sheetCapitalising operating leases adds both a ROU asset and a lease liability to the balance sheet. This increases both total assets AND total debt — affecting D/E ratio, ROA, and Net Debt/EBITDA. Always strip lease liability for clean leverage analysis in retail/airlines.
💡
Always read the notes, not just the face of B/SThe notes contain: contingent liabilities, related-party transactions, segment data, key accounting policies, and breakdown of long-term investments. The most important information in a balance sheet is often in the notes.
💡
Track CWIP carefully for capex-cycle companiesLarge CWIP balances are future revenue generators — but they earn nothing until commissioned. Compare CWIP as % of gross block over time: peak CWIP = peak capex cycle. Commissioning CWIP → revenue + depreciation charge both kick in.
💡
Negative equity is not always distressIn an LBO, equity can be structurally negative from dividend recaps. Platform businesses with deferred revenue can show negative equity. Context: is it structural (business model) or distress (accumulated losses)?
The Cash Flow Statement shows actual cash movement in three categories: Operations, Investing, and Financing. Unlike the P&L, it cannot be manipulated through accrual accounting — making it the most reliable statement for assessing financial health. Starts with Net Income and reconciles to actual cash.
Cash Flow from Operations (CFO)
Start with Net Income (PAT)
(+) Depreciation & Amortisation Non-cash charge — added back
(+) Stock-Based Compensation (ESOP) Non-cash expense — added back
(+/−) Deferred Tax Non-cash; adds back DTL, deducts DTA
(+/−) Change in Working Capital ↑ A/R or Inventory = cash outflow (−)
↑ A/P or Deferred Revenue = cash inflow (+)
(+/−) Other Non-cash Items Provisions, impairments, write-offs
= Cash Flow from Operations True operating cash generation
Cash Flow from Investing (CFI)
(−) Purchase of PP&E (Capex) Core capex — maintenance + growth
(−) Purchase of Investments Acquisition of subsidiaries, JVs, equity stakes
(+) Proceeds from Asset Sales Disposal of PP&E, investments, subsidiaries
(+/−) Change in Investments Net purchase/redemption of financial investments
= Cash Flow from Investing Usually negative (growth requires investment)
Cash Flow from Financing (CFF)
(+) Proceeds from Debt Raised New loans, bonds, debentures
(−) Repayment of Debt Principal repayment of borrowings
(+) Equity Raised IPO, rights issue, QIP proceeds
(−) Dividends Paid Cash dividend to shareholders
(−) Buyback of Shares Share repurchase from market
(−) Lease Payments (Ind AS 116) Principal portion of lease liability repayment
= Cash Flow from Financing Reflects capital structure decisions
Key Formulas & Metrics
Free Cash Flow (FCF)= CFO − Capex Unlevered FCF (FCFF)= EBIT × (1−t) + D&A − Capex − ΔNWC Levered FCF (FCFE)= Net Income + D&A − Capex − ΔNWC + Net Borrowings FCF Yield= FCF ÷ Market Cap × 100 FCF / PAT> 0.8x = high earnings quality CFO / EBITDA> 0.7x = healthy cash conversion Net Change in Cash = CFO + CFI + CFFClosing Cash = Opening Cash + Net Change in Cash
Cash Flow Quality Signals
CFO > PAT ✓ Strong — working capital release or non-cash add-backs exceeding D&A
CFO < PAT consistently ✗ Concern — earnings may not be converting to cash; check WC
CFO negative, PAT positive ✗ Red flag — possible channel stuffing or aggressive revenue recognition
High capex / CFO ratio Capital-intensive growth phase; track when capex normalises for FCF inflection
Consistent positive FCF ✓ Premium quality — compound machines (Asian Paints, Pidilite, TCS)
CFI inflows > outflows Company is selling assets to fund operations — often a stress signal
3-Statement Linkages
How the Three Statements Connect
Net Income (P&L)→Top line of Cash Flow Statement (CFO starting point) + increases Retained Earnings on Balance Sheet
D&A (P&L expense)→Added back in CFO (non-cash) + reduces PP&E on Balance Sheet (accumulated depreciation)
Change in A/R (B/S)→Working capital adjustment in CFO: A/R ↑ = cash outflow; A/R ↓ = cash inflow
Capex (CFI outflow)→Increases PP&E on Balance Sheet + future D&A on P&L (creates a depreciation tail)
Debt raised (CFF inflow)→Increases Cash on Balance Sheet + increases Debt liability on Balance Sheet
Dividends paid (CFF outflow)→Reduces Cash on Balance Sheet + reduces Retained Earnings on Balance Sheet
Net Change in Cash (CFS)→Reconciles to closing Cash balance on Balance Sheet (Opening Cash + Net Change = Closing Cash)
Deferred Tax (P&L)→Non-cash; added back in CFO + creates DTL/DTA on Balance Sheet
Common Pitfalls & Analyst Tips
⚠
Mistaking "cash profit" for free cash flowCash profit = PAT + D&A. FCF = CFO − Capex. These are very different. A company with ₹500 Cr cash profit but ₹600 Cr capex has negative FCF and is burning cash despite being "profitable".
⚠
Ignoring maintenance vs growth capexTotal capex includes maintenance (keeping assets running) and growth (adding capacity). Only growth capex is discretionary. Companies that classify maintenance capex as growth to show higher FCF are inflating free cash flow quality.
💡
Use CFO/PAT as your first earnings quality checkA ratio consistently above 1.0x means the company collects more cash than it reports as profit — excellent quality. Below 0.7x for multiple years = investigate working capital, revenue recognition, or provisioning policy.
💡
Normalise capex for the cycleCapex is lumpy — a company mid-expansion shows depressed FCF. Model a "normalised capex" (maintenance + steady-state growth) to get through-the-cycle FCF. This is what drives long-term valuation in a DCF.
Efficiency ratios measure how well a company uses its assets and manages its working capital cycle.
Ratio Formula What It Measures Good Range Red Flag India Context
Asset Turnover Revenue ÷ Avg Total Assets Revenue generated per ₹ of assets. Higher = more efficient use of asset base. 1.0x–2.0x (manufacturing)
2.0x–4.0x (retail/services)		 <0.5x signals asset-heavy business or underutilisation DMart: ~3.5x (very efficient). Capital-heavy cos like NTPC: ~0.3x — normal for utilities.
Inventory Turnover COGS ÷ Avg Inventory How many times inventory is sold and replaced in a period. Higher = faster-moving stock. 6x–12x (FMCG/retail)
3x–6x (manufacturing)		 <3x may indicate obsolete/slow-moving inventory DMart targets ~30 inventory days (12x turnover). Fashion retail: 60–90 days normal.
Inventory Days (DIO) 365 ÷ Inventory Turnover
or (Avg Inventory ÷ COGS) × 365		 Average days to sell through inventory. Lower = faster conversion to cash. 30–45 days (value retail)
60–90 days (fashion/pharma)		 >120 days in FMCG = serious concern Track trend vs peers. Sudden DIO spike = demand slowdown or channel stuffing.
Receivables Turnover Revenue ÷ Avg Accounts Receivable How quickly the company collects cash from customers. Higher = faster collection. 8x–15x (B2C businesses)
4x–8x (B2B businesses)		 <4x in B2C = potential revenue recognition issue B2C cos (FMCG, retail) should have very low receivables. High A/R in B2C = red flag.
Debtor Days (DSO) 365 ÷ Receivables Turnover
or (Avg A/R ÷ Revenue) × 365		 Average days to collect payment after a sale. Lower = better cash collection. 15–30 days (B2C)
45–75 days (B2B/infra)		 Rising DSO faster than revenue growth = collections issue IT services: 65–80 days normal. Infrastructure/EPC: 90–120 days. DISCOM payments: often 120+ days (risk).
Payable Days (DPO) (Avg Accounts Payable ÷ COGS) × 365 Average days to pay suppliers. Higher = company holds cash longer (working capital advantage). 30–60 days (FMCG)
60–90 days (retail/auto)		 >120 days may signal liquidity stress or supplier disputes DMart pays quickly (low DPO) — but buys at deep discount. Large FMCG cos use DPO as implicit financing.
Cash Conversion Cycle (CCC) DIO + DSO − DPO Days between paying for inventory and collecting cash from customers. Lower (or negative) = better. 0–30 days (strong businesses)
Negative = working capital advantage 		Rising CCC = deteriorating working capital management DMart: ~10 days. Zomato/Swiggy: negative CCC (collect before paying). EPC/infra companies: 120–180 days.
Fixed Asset Turnover Revenue ÷ Avg Net Fixed Assets (PP&E) Revenue per ₹ of fixed assets. Measures how productively the company uses its physical assets. 2x–5x (manufacturing)
5x–10x (asset-light services)		 <1x suggests overcapacity or underutilisation of capex IT services: 8–12x (asset-light). Steel/cement: 0.8–1.2x (capex-heavy, normal). Track post-expansion recovery.
Working Capital Turnover Revenue ÷ Avg Working Capital Revenue generated per ₹ of net working capital deployed. Higher = more efficient WC use. 5x–15x (efficient businesses) Negative WC turnover not meaningful — use CCC instead Negative WC businesses (DMart, Zomato) have infinite WC turnover — a structural advantage.
Liquidity ratios assess the company's ability to meet short-term obligations with short-term assets. Critical for credit analysis and distress detection.
Ratio Formula What It Measures Good Range Red Flag India Context
Current Ratio Current Assets ÷ Current Liabilities Ability to cover short-term liabilities with short-term assets. Most widely used liquidity measure. 1.5x–2.5x (manufacturing)
1.0x–1.5x (retail/FMCG)		 <1.0x = current liabilities exceed current assets — potential liquidity stress D-Mart current ratio ~0.7x — not a problem because of negative WC model. Context matters enormously.
Quick Ratio (Acid Test) (Current Assets − Inventory) ÷ Current Liabilities More conservative than current ratio — excludes inventory which may not be quickly liquidated. 0.8x–1.5x (healthy)
>1.0x preferred for manufacturing		 <0.5x in a manufacturing company = serious short-term liquidity concern Better metric than current ratio for companies with large slow-moving inventory (pharma, auto components).
Cash Ratio (Cash + Cash Equivalents) ÷ Current Liabilities Most conservative liquidity metric — only counts actual cash, not receivables or inventory. 0.2x–0.5x (normal)
>0.5x = very strong liquidity		 <0.1x leaves little buffer for unexpected cash needs Tech/platform companies often hold large cash buffers. Check against upcoming debt maturities, not just CL.
Operating Cash Flow Ratio Operating Cash Flow ÷ Current Liabilities Measures ability to cover current liabilities from operating cash flows (not just balance sheet assets). >0.4x considered healthy
>1.0x = very strong		 <0.2x means the business struggles to fund its own short-term obligations from operations More dynamic than current ratio. Useful for companies with large deferred revenue or seasonality in WC.
Net Working Capital (₹) Current Assets − Current Liabilities Absolute rupee buffer available after settling all current liabilities. Positive = cushion; negative = deficit. Positive and growing with revenue for most businesses Rapidly declining NWC without explanation = cash being consumed Negative NWC is fine for Zomato, DMart (structural). Negative NWC for an EPC company = distress signal.
Defensive Interval Ratio (Cash + Marketable Securities + Net Receivables) ÷ Daily Operating Expenses How many days a company can operate using only its most liquid assets, without any new revenue. >60 days considered healthy <30 days = very limited buffer; vulnerable to revenue disruption Particularly useful for companies with lumpy revenue (project-based cos, real estate developers).
Solvency ratios assess the company's long-term financial health and ability to meet long-term debt obligations. Critical for credit rating and leveraged buyout analysis.
Ratio Formula What It Measures Good Range Red Flag India Context
Debt-to-Equity (D/E) Total Debt ÷ Shareholders' Equity Financial leverage. How much debt is used relative to equity. Higher = more leveraged, more risk. <1.0x (conservative)
1.0x–2.0x (moderate leverage)		 >3.0x in non-financial companies = high risk; >5x = distress territory Capital-intensive sectors (power, infra): 2–4x normal. NBFCs: 4–7x by design (leverage is the business model).
Net Debt / EBITDA (Total Debt − Cash) ÷ EBITDA Years of EBITDA needed to repay net debt. Most commonly used leverage metric in M&A and credit analysis. <2.0x (investment grade)
2.0x–3.5x (moderate)
3.5x–5.0x (leveraged)		 >5.0x = highly leveraged; >7.0x = distress (Vodafone Idea: 8x+) Telecom (Airtel): 2.5–3x target. RE developers: 3–5x. Adani Green: 5–6x acceptable given PPA-backed revenue.
Interest Coverage Ratio (ICR) EBIT ÷ Interest Expense
also: EBITDA ÷ Interest Expense		 How many times the company can cover its interest payments from operating profit. Higher = safer. >3.0x (investment grade comfort)
>5.0x (very healthy)		 <1.5x = potential interest payment risk; <1.0x = cannot cover interest RBI/rating agencies watch ICR closely. Sub-2x ICR in India often triggers covenant breaches and NPA classification.
Debt Service Coverage (DSCR) Operating Cash Flow ÷ (Principal Repayment + Interest) Ability to service total debt obligations (principal + interest) from operating cash flows. Used by lenders. >1.25x required by most lenders
>1.5x considered comfortable 		<1.0x = cash flow insufficient to service debt — default risk Infrastructure project finance in India mandates minimum DSCR of 1.2–1.3x in loan covenants.
Debt-to-Assets Total Debt ÷ Total Assets Proportion of assets financed by debt. Shows financial risk from a balance sheet perspective. <0.4x (conservative)
0.4x–0.6x (moderate)		 >0.7x = majority of assets debt-financed; limited equity cushion Asset-heavy sectors (power, steel): 0.5–0.65x normal. Pure services / IT: should be well below 0.2x.
Equity Multiplier Total Assets ÷ Shareholders' Equity Financial leverage from a DuPont perspective. Higher = more assets financed by debt. Component of ROE. 1.5x–2.5x (balanced)
NBFCs/banks: 5x–10x (by design)		 >4x for industrials = significant leverage risk Central to DuPont ROE decomposition: ROE = Net Margin × Asset Turnover × Equity Multiplier.
Fixed Charge Coverage (EBIT + Fixed Charges) ÷ (Fixed Charges + Interest) Ability to cover ALL fixed obligations (interest + lease payments) from earnings. More complete than ICR. >2.0x healthy
>3.0x very comfortable		 <1.2x = high fixed-cost risk, especially in downturns Key for retail (heavy lease obligations) and airlines. Post Ind AS 116, lease liabilities are now on balance sheet.
Promoter Pledge Ratio Pledged shares ÷ Total promoter holding (%) India-specific solvency signal. Pledging shares for loans — if stock falls, forced selling can cascade. <10% pledging = low risk
0% = cleanest signal		 >30% pledge = significant risk; >50% = avoid SEBI mandates disclosure. High promoter pledge + high D/E = double leverage risk unique to Indian markets.
Profitability ratios measure how efficiently a company generates profit from revenue, assets, and equity. Core to any equity research model.
Ratio Formula What It Measures Good Range Red Flag India Context
Gross Margin % (Revenue − COGS) ÷ Revenue × 100 Profitability after direct production costs. Reflects pricing power and input cost management. FMCG: 45–60%
IT services: 30–35%
Retail (value): 14–18%		 Declining gross margin with rising revenue = input cost inflation not passed through HUL gross margin: ~50%. DMart: ~15% (intentionally low — volume model). Nestle India: ~55%.
EBITDA Margin % EBITDA ÷ Revenue × 100 Operating profitability before capital structure, tax, and non-cash items. Most-used margin in equity research. FMCG: 20–28%
IT services: 18–26%
Telecom: 48–55%
Retail: 8–12% 		EBITDA margin contraction >200bps YoY without explanation = earnings quality concern Note Ind AS 116 impact: lease cos show inflated EBITDA. Always compare pre/post Ind AS 116 for retail/airlines.
EBIT Margin (Operating Margin) % EBIT ÷ Revenue × 100 Profitability after operating expenses AND depreciation/amortisation. Better than EBITDA for capex-heavy cos. IT: 20–25%
Pharma: 18–24%
Auto OEM: 8–12%		 EBIT margin < cost of debt means operations cannot cover interest — structural problem Use EBIT margin over EBITDA margin for companies with high capex (power, steel, telecom).
Net Profit Margin (PAT Margin) % PAT ÷ Revenue × 100 Bottom-line profitability after all costs, interest, and taxes. Affected by capital structure. FMCG: 14–18%
IT services: 14–20%
Banking: ROA metric preferred 		PAT margin rising while EBITDA margin falling = financial engineering (other income, low tax) Watch 'other income': treasury gains, asset sales, subsidiary dividends can inflate PAT without operational improvement.
Return on Equity (ROE) PAT ÷ Avg Shareholders' Equity × 100 Return generated for equity shareholders. DuPont = Net Margin × Asset Turnover × Equity Multiplier. >15% = good
>20% = excellent
>25% = franchise quality 		High ROE driven by high leverage (equity multiplier) — not the same as operational excellence HDFC Bank: ~16% ROE. Asian Paints: ~25%. Always check if ROE is leverage-driven or margin/turnover-driven.
Return on Capital Employed (ROCE) EBIT ÷ Capital Employed × 100
Capital Employed = Equity + Debt (or Total Assets − CL)		 Return on ALL capital (debt + equity). ROCE > WACC = value creation. Best measure of capital efficiency. >15% = value-creating
>20% = strong moat
>25% = exceptional		 ROCE < WACC = destroying shareholder value even if PAT is positive Asian Paints: 35%+. Pidilite: 28%+. Consistent ROCE improvement = re-rating catalyst in Indian markets.
Return on Assets (ROA) PAT ÷ Avg Total Assets × 100 Profit generated per ₹ of assets. Primary profitability metric for banks (where leverage is structural). Non-financial: >8%
Banks: >1.0% (ROA)
NBFC: >2.5%		 Bank ROA <0.5% = weak profitability; <0% = destroying asset value HDFC Bank ROA: ~1.9%. SBI: ~1.0% (improved). Kotak: ~2.1%. Premium bank valuations require sustained ROA >1.5%.
Return on Invested Capital (ROIC) NOPAT ÷ Invested Capital × 100
NOPAT = EBIT × (1−tax); IC = Equity + Debt − Cash 		Most rigorous return metric — excludes excess cash from denominator, uses after-tax operating profit. >12% = healthy
>20% = strong competitive advantage		 ROIC < WACC = every ₹ invested destroys value — a sell signal regardless of growth Best used for capital-allocation decisions. Buy companies where ROIC is > WACC and the gap is widening.
Contribution Margin % (Revenue − Variable Costs) ÷ Revenue × 100 Revenue left after variable costs to cover fixed costs and generate profit. Critical for internet/new-age cos. >0% = unit economics viable
>20% = building toward EBITDA profitability		 Negative contribution margin = losing money on every transaction — structural business model failure Zomato food delivery contribution margin turned positive in FY23 — a key re-rating milestone. Blinkit followed in FY24.
Valuation ratios determine how the market prices a stock relative to its fundamentals. Used in trading comps, precedent transactions, and relative value investing.
Ratio Formula What It Measures Typical Range When to Use / Avoid India Context
P/E Ratio Market Price per Share ÷ EPS
or Market Cap ÷ PAT		 How much investors pay per ₹ of earnings. Most widely quoted valuation multiple. FMCG: 45–60x
IT: 20–30x
Banks: 15–25x
Cyclicals: 8–15x		 Use for: stable earnings businesses. Avoid for: cos with negative PAT, cyclicals at trough earnings, high D&A businesses. Nifty50 long-run avg P/E: ~20–22x. Premium cos (Asian Paints, Nestlé): 60–80x P/E — justified by ROCE > 25%.
EV / EBITDA Enterprise Value ÷ EBITDA
EV = Mkt Cap + Net Debt + Minority Interest		 Capital-structure-neutral valuation. Most used multiple in M&A and equity research for non-financial companies. FMCG: 30–50x
IT: 18–28x
Telecom: 8–12x
Manufacturing: 8–15x 		Use for: most non-financial companies. Avoid for: banks/NBFCs (debt is product), negative EBITDA businesses. Airtel EV/EBITDA: ~10x. HUL: ~45x. The gap reflects earnings quality, growth, and capital intensity differences.
EV / EBIT Enterprise Value ÷ EBIT Better than EV/EBITDA for capital-intensive businesses — accounts for D&A as a real economic cost. IT: 22–32x
Pharma: 20–30x
Auto: 15–20x		 Use when: comparing capex-heavy vs asset-light businesses. Avoids EBITDA's bias toward capex-heavy companies. Essential for comparing IT services vs BPO — depreciation treatment differs significantly by model.
EV / Revenue (EV/Sales) Enterprise Value ÷ Revenue Revenue-based valuation — used when EBITDA is negative or for early-stage/high-growth companies. SaaS/tech: 5–15x
FMCG: 4–8x
IT: 3–5x
New-age internet: 2–8x 		Use for: pre-profit cos, revenue-stage startups, hypergrowth businesses. Avoid for: mature, low-margin businesses. Zomato valued at ~5x EV/Revenue in FY24. Compare to Doordash (US) at similar stage — calibrate for India premium/discount.
P/B (Price-to-Book) Market Cap ÷ Book Value of Equity
or Share Price ÷ Book Value per Share		 Market value vs accounting book value. Primary valuation multiple for banks and financial companies. Banks: 1.5–4x (premium)
PSU banks: 0.7–1.2x
NBFCs: 2–5x		 Use for: banks, NBFCs, insurance, asset-heavy industrials. Avoid for: asset-light services (book value meaningless). HDFC Bank: ~3.0x P/B. Kotak: ~3.5x. SBI: ~1.2x. P/B × ROE relationship: higher sustainable ROE justifies higher P/B.
PEG Ratio P/E ÷ EPS Growth Rate (%) Adjusts P/E for growth. A PEG of 1.0x = P/E equals growth rate — considered fair value by Peter Lynch rule. <1.0x = potentially undervalued
1.0x = fairly valued
>2.0x = expensive relative to growth		 Use for: high-growth companies where raw P/E looks stretched. Avoid for: cyclicals, negative growth, single-product cos. A Nifty FMCG stock at 60x P/E with 20% EPS CAGR = PEG of 3.0x — expensive. At 15% CAGR = PEG 4.0x — very expensive.
Dividend Yield % Annual Dividend per Share ÷ Market Price × 100 Cash return to shareholders from dividends as a % of market price. Relevant for income investors. 1–3% = normal equity yield
>4% = high yield (PSU stocks)		 Use for: mature PSU companies (Coal India, ONGC), dividend-oriented strategies. Avoid as primary valuation for growth cos. Coal India: ~8–10% yield. ITC: ~3–4%. High dividend yield can signal either value or value trap — check payout sustainability.
EV / Capacity (Sector-Specific) EV ÷ Operational Capacity (MW, tonnes, beds, subscribers) Used when EBITDA is negative or not meaningful — values the business on operational scale. Power: $0.8–1.5mn/MW
Cement: $90–140/tonne
Telecom: $200–400/sub 		Use for: greenfield projects pre-revenue, capital projects, asset-heavy sector comps. Not for mature profitable businesses. Adani Green: ~$1.2mn/MW operational. Cement: UltraTech at ₹15,000–18,000 Cr/mn tonne EV — key M&A benchmark.
Earnings Yield % EPS ÷ Market Price × 100
(inverse of P/E ratio)		 Compares equity return to bond yields. If earnings yield > 10-yr G-Sec yield → equity is cheaper than bonds. Earnings yield > G-Sec yield = equities attractively valued on relative basis Use for: macro asset allocation decisions (equity vs bonds). Basis of the Fed Model applied to India. Nifty earnings yield vs 10yr G-Sec: when gap narrows to <1%, equities historically look expensive. Current 10yr: ~7.1%.

Modelling
⌨️ Excel Shortcuts
✅ Best Practices
⚠️ Mistakes
🔗 3-Statement
💰 Debt Schedule
🔄 Circularity
Valuation
📊 Trading Comps
🤝 Precedent Txns
🔨 DCF Build
⚖️ WACC
🏁 Terminal Value
📐 Sensitivity
🏈 Football Field
A DCF values a business by discounting its future free cash flows to the present. It is the only intrinsic valuation method — not anchored to market prices. Master the build process, not just the theory.
Step-by-Step DCF Build
1
Project Revenue (3–5 years explicit period)
Use the sector-specific revenue model (volume × price, NII × NIM, GMV × take rate). Build a bottom-up forecast — never just apply a flat growth % to revenue. Justify each assumption. Typical explicit period: 5 years for stable cos, 7–10 years for high-growth.
2
Build down to EBIT, then NOPAT
Revenue → Gross Profit → EBITDA → EBIT. Then: NOPAT = EBIT × (1 − tax rate). Use the marginal tax rate (India: 25.17% new regime). NOPAT is the after-tax operating profit available to all capital providers.
3
Calculate Unlevered Free Cash Flow (FCFF)
FCFF = NOPAT + D&A − ΔWorking Capital − Capex
D&A is added back (non-cash). ΔWC: increase = cash outflow (negative). Capex: both maintenance and growth. FCFF is capital-structure neutral — goes to EV, not equity value.
4
Calculate WACC (discount rate)
WACC = Ke × (E/V) + Kd × (D/V) × (1−t)
Use target capital structure (not current if temporarily distorted). India: typical WACC range 10–14% for large caps. See WACC tab for full build.
5
Discount each year's FCFF to present value
PV(FCFFₙ) = FCFFₙ ÷ (1 + WACC)ⁿ
Use mid-year convention if cash flows are generated throughout the year (not just at year end): discount factor = (1+WACC)^(n−0.5). Sum all PV(FCFF) = PV of explicit period.
6
Calculate Terminal Value
Two methods — see Terminal Value tab. TV typically = 60–80% of total EV. Discount TV back: PV(TV) = TV ÷ (1+WACC)ⁿ
7
Sum to Enterprise Value → Equity Value → Price
EV = Σ PV(FCFF) + PV(TV)
Equity Value = EV − Net Debt − Minority Interest + Cash
Price per share = Equity Value ÷ Diluted shares
8
Sanity check & sensitivity
Check: TV% of EV (should be 60–80%). Run WACC × terminal growth sensitivity table. Cross-check implied EV/EBITDA vs trading comps. If implied multiple looks absurd — your assumptions are wrong.
FCFF Formula — Detailed
FCFF= EBIT × (1 − Tax Rate) + Depreciation & Amortisation ← add back (non-cash)− Capital Expenditure ← cash out for assets− Increase in Net Working Capital ← cash tied up in WCEquivalently, starting from CFO:FCFF= CFO + Interest Expense × (1−t) − Capex Levered FCF (FCFE → Equity Value):FCFE= FCFF − Interest ×(1−t) + Net Borrowings
Key DCF Assumptions — India Benchmarks
Input Typical Range Note
Explicit period 5–10 years Longer for high-growth; shorter for stable
WACC (large cap) 10–14% Higher for cyclicals, lower for regulated utilities
Terminal growth (g) 4–6% nominal India GDP growth; g must be < WACC always
Tax rate 25.17% New regime; use marginal not effective
TV as % of EV 60–80% >90% = too dependent on assumptions
Mid-year convention Recommended More accurate for ongoing businesses
When NOT to Use a DCF
Banks & NBFCs Debt is their raw material — FCFF is meaningless. Use DDM or P/BV × ROE instead
Pre-revenue startups No cash flows to forecast. Use EV/GMV, EV/Users, market comparables
Highly cyclical cos Mid-cycle FCF is distorted. Normalise earnings first or use EV/mid-cycle EBITDA
Distressed companies Going-concern risk changes cost of capital entirely. Use liquidation or restructuring analysis
Asset holding cos Use NAV (Net Asset Value) — sum of component market values
Common DCF Mistakes
⚠
Using current capital structure for WACCIf a company is temporarily over-levered (post-acquisition) or under-levered, its current D/E ratio is not representative. Use the target long-run capital structure or industry average D/E in your WACC.
⚠
Terminal growth rate higher than WACCIf g ≥ WACC the Gordon Growth Model gives a negative or infinite TV — a mathematical impossibility. g must always be less than WACC. In India, g = 5–6% is standard; anything above 7% requires strong justification.
⚠
Ignoring capex in terminal yearMany analysts use final year FCFF as the TV base but forget that a growing business needs capex to sustain growth. Reinvestment rate = g ÷ ROIC. Adjust TV base for reinvestment: FCFFₙ₊₁ = NOPAT × (1 − g/ROIC)
💡
Always run a reverse DCF firstBefore building your own projections, run a reverse DCF: what growth rate is the current market price implying at a reasonable WACC? If the market implies 25% revenue CAGR for 10 years for a stable FMCG company — that's a sell signal.
💡
Cross-check implied EV/EBITDA exit multipleYour terminal value implies an exit multiple. Calculate it: TV ÷ Terminal year EBITDA. If it's 45x for a commodity business — your g is too high. This cross-check catches unreasonable terminal assumptions instantly.
💡
Normalise the final year before applying TVThe last explicit period year should represent a "normalised" steady state — not a peak capex year, not a WC disruption year. If year 5 looks abnormal, extend to year 7 or adjust the terminal FCF directly.
WACC is the blended required return for all capital providers (equity + debt). It is the discount rate in a DCF and the hurdle rate for value creation (ROIC > WACC = value created). Getting WACC wrong by 1% moves your DCF price target by 10–20%.
WACC Formula — Full Build
WACC= Ke × (E/V) + Kd × (D/V) × (1−t) Where: Ke= Cost of Equity (via CAPM) Kd= Pre-tax Cost of Debt E/V= Equity ÷ (Equity + Debt) ← use market valuesD/V= Debt ÷ (Equity + Debt) t= Marginal tax rate ← India: 25.17%Debt gets a tax shield: interest is tax-deductiblePreferred stock: add Kp × (P/V) if material
Cost of Equity — CAPM:Ke= Rf + β × ERP Rf= Risk-free rate ← India 10-yr G-Sec: ~7.1%β= Levered Beta of the stock ERP= Equity Risk Premium ← India: 5.5–7%Example: Rf=7.1%, β=1.2, ERP=6.0%Ke= 7.1 + 1.2 × 6.0 =14.3%
How to Build Beta — Step by Step
1
Get raw (levered) beta
Run regression of stock returns vs Nifty 50 over 2–5 years (weekly data). Or use published beta from Bloomberg, NSE, or Damodaran's dataset.
2
Unlever the beta (remove capital structure effect)
βᵤ = βₗ ÷ [1 + (1−t) × (D/E)]
This gives the "asset beta" — pure business risk without leverage distortion.
3
Relever at target capital structure
βₗ = βᵤ × [1 + (1−t) × (D/E)_target]
Use target D/E, not current. This gives a forward-looking beta reflecting the intended capital structure.
4
Use median of peer betas for private cos
For private companies or newly listed stocks with insufficient history, use the median unlevered beta of 5–8 comparable listed peers, then relever at the target D/E.
WACC — India Benchmarks by Sector
Sector Typical WACC Key Driver
FMCG / Consumer 10–12% Low beta (0.6–0.8), low leverage, stable cash flows
IT Services 11–13% Beta ~0.8–1.0, minimal debt, USD revenue exposure
Pharma 11–13% Moderate beta, FDA risk premium sometimes added
Auto / Manufacturing 12–14% Cyclical beta 1.0–1.3, moderate leverage
Real Estate 13–16% High beta, high leverage, illiquidity premium
Power / Renewables 9–11% PPA-backed low risk; high leverage but regulated
Internet / New-age 14–18% High beta 1.3–1.8, pre-profit, higher risk premium
Cost of Debt — How to Estimate
Kd Build Methods
Method How Best for
YTM on traded bonds Yield-to-maturity on company's outstanding listed bonds Large cos with listed NCDs/bonds
Interest expense / avg debt Reported interest expense ÷ average total debt balance Quick estimate from annual report
Credit spread + Rf Risk-free rate + credit spread for company's rating (AAA: +40bps, AA: +80bps, BBB: +200bps) When bond YTM unavailable
Marginal borrowing rate Rate on most recently taken loan (in notes to accounts) Forward-looking; most accurate
⚠
Using book value weights instead of market valueWACC requires market value weights for E and D, not book values. Book equity can be massively different from market cap. Always use: E = market cap, D = total financial debt at face value (approximation for market value of debt).
⚠
Using effective tax rate instead of marginalThe tax shield benefit on debt uses the marginal tax rate (the rate on the next rupee of income), not the effective rate (which averages in tax breaks, deferred taxes, and exemptions). India marginal = 25.17% under new regime.
💡
Add a size or illiquidity premium for mid/small capsCAPM underestimates required return for small/mid-cap stocks. Add 0.5–2% size premium to Ke for companies outside Nifty 50. Damodaran publishes India size premiums annually — use his latest dataset.
Terminal Value (TV) captures the value of all cash flows beyond the explicit forecast period — typically 60–80% of total EV. Getting TV right is more important than getting your Year 3 forecast right. Two methods: Gordon Growth Model and Exit Multiple Method.
Method 1 — Gordon Growth Model (Perpetuity)
TV= FCFFₙ × (1 + g) ÷ (WACC − g) Where: FCFFₙ= Final explicit year normalised FCFF g= Perpetual growth rate ← India: 4–6%WACC= Discount rate Critical constraint: g MUST be less than WACCg should not exceed long-run nominal GDP growthIndia nominal GDP ≈ real GDP (7%) + inflation (4–5%) ≈ 11–12%Conservative: use 4–6% for most listed cosPV(TV)= TV ÷ (1 + WACC)n
g assumption What it implies Appropriate for
3–4% Mature, slow-growth business, roughly inflation-level growth PSUs, commodities, utilities
4–5% Stable growth inline with economy FMCG, pharma, IT services
5–6% Slight above-GDP perpetual growth Premium consumer, financials
>7% Assumes company grows faster than India GDP forever — rarely justified Very rarely; needs strong moat case
Method 2 — Exit Multiple Method
TV= Final Year EBITDA × Exit EV/EBITDA Multiple Exit multiple = median of current peer trading multiplesUse NTM (forward) EBITDA to avoid double-counting growthPV(TV)= TV ÷ (1 + WACC)nImplied g check: TV = FCFFₙ₊₁ ÷ (WACC − g)Solve for g → sanity check vs GGM assumption
Sector Exit EV/EBITDA Exit P/E
FMCG 28–40x 40–55x
IT Services 18–25x 22–30x
Pharma 16–22x 22–28x
Auto OEM 10–14x 18–22x
Banking N/A (use P/BV) 14–20x
Power / Utilities 8–12x 12–18x
Real Estate 14–20x 20–28x
GGM vs Exit Multiple — Which to Use?
Gordon Growth Model Better for stable, mature businesses. Theoretically grounded. More sensitive to assumptions.
Exit Multiple More common in banking practice. Market-anchored. Assumes market is fairly valued at exit.
Best practice Use both and cross-check. If they diverge significantly, understand why before publishing.
TV Sanity Checks — Run These Every Time
Check 1 — TV % of EV
TV% of EV= PV(TV) ÷ Total EV Healthy range: 60–80% Concern: 80–90% Red flag: >90% If TV > 90% of EV: extend the explicit period, or your Year 1–5 FCFs are too low.
Check 2 — Implied Exit Multiple
Implied multiple= TV ÷ Final yr EBITDA If GGM TV implies 60x EV/EBITDA for a commodity business → g is too high. Target: implied exit multiple should be close to current peer multiples. Significant premium needs justification.
Check 3 — Implied g from Exit Multiple
Solve: TV = FCFFₙ₊₁ ÷ (WACC − g) g= WACC − (FCFFₙ₊₁ ÷ TV) If your exit multiple implies g = 9% for a mature co → multiple is too high. Cross-checks GGM and exit multiple approaches against each other.
A sensitivity table shows how your output (share price or EV) changes when you vary two key inputs simultaneously. It is the most important output of any DCF — a single price target is meaningless without showing the range of outcomes under different assumptions.
How to Build a Sensitivity Table
1
Choose the 2 most impactful variables
For DCF: almost always WACC × Terminal Growth Rate. Alternatively: WACC × Exit Multiple, or Revenue CAGR × EBITDA Margin. Pick variables that your reader most wants to stress-test.
2
Set ranges — ±2 steps from base case
WACC: base ±100–200bps in 50bps steps. Terminal g: base ±1–2% in 0.5% steps. Creates a 5×5 matrix (25 scenarios). Always include your base case at the centre.
3
In Excel: use Data Table (What-If Analysis)
Set up row input cell (WACC) and column input cell (g). Link the table output to your implied share price cell. Excel recalculates all 25 combinations automatically.
4
Apply conditional formatting
Green = above current price (upside). Red = below current price (downside). Circle or bold the base case. This lets a reader instantly see the bull/bear/base distribution.
Example: WACC × Terminal Growth — Implied Share Price (₹)
Row: WACC  |  Column: Terminal Growth Rate  |  ■ Base case  ■ Bull  ■ Bear
WACC \ g 3.5% 4.0% 4.5% 5.0% 5.5%
9.0% ₹1,840 ₹2,020 ₹2,240 ₹2,510 ₹2,860
10.0% ₹1,520 ₹1,640 ₹1,780 ₹1,950 ₹2,160
10.5% ◀ base ₹1,380 ₹1,480 ₹1,590 ◀ ₹1,730 ₹1,900
11.0% ₹1,250 ₹1,330 ₹1,430 ₹1,540 ₹1,680
12.0% ₹1,040 ₹1,100 ₹1,170 ₹1,250 ₹1,340
Current price: ₹1,590 (base). Bull = above current price. Bear = below. Range: ₹1,040–₹2,860 — always present this range to investors, never just the base case price.
Other Useful Sensitivity Pairs by Sector
Sensitivity Variable Pairs — India Equity Research
Sector Primary Variable Secondary Variable Why This Pair
FMCG Revenue CAGR (FY25–28) EBITDA Margin % Volume recovery vs input cost normalisation are the two biggest swing factors
IT Services USD Revenue CAGR EBIT Margin % Demand environment (growth) and cost/utilisation (margin) drive earnings independently
Banking NIM % (FY26) Credit Cost % (FY26) NIM and credit cost are the two levers that determine PAT — stress-test both simultaneously
Real Estate Presales CAGR Realization/sqft growth Volume (presales) and pricing are often inversely correlated — useful to stress-test together
Telecom ARPU (₹/month, FY26) Subscriber growth % ARPU hike magnitude and subscriber retention are the two variables the market debates most
Power / RE Tariff (₹/kWh) Capacity additions (MW) Merchant tariff volatility and capex execution drive EV in RE companies
A Football Field chart visualises the valuation range from multiple methodologies side-by-side on a single price axis. It shows investors where each method lands and how the current market price compares to intrinsic/relative value.
How to Build a Football Field
1
Run all valuation methodologies
At minimum: DCF (with sensitivity range), Trading Comps (EV/EBITDA and P/E), and Precedent Transactions. Optionally add: 52-week range, analyst consensus target, LBO floor.
2
For each method, derive a min–max range
DCF: bear WACC × low g to bull WACC × high g. Comps: apply 25th–75th percentile multiple to your estimates. Transactions: apply discount–premium range.
3
Plot as horizontal bar chart
Each bar represents one methodology. X-axis = share price. Bar spans from min to max implied price. Add a vertical line for current market price. Methodology with the widest range = most uncertain.
4
Draw conclusions — where does market price sit?
If current price is below all ranges → strong buy signal. If above all ranges → expensive. If near the middle of DCF range but above comps → market pricing in growth that comps don't capture.
Example Football Field — FMCG Company (₹ per share)
Vertical line = current market price ₹1,590
₹800₹1,000₹1,200₹1,400₹1,600₹1,800₹2,000₹2,200
DCF (WACC sens.)
₹1,040 — ₹2,160
EV/EBITDA Comps
₹1,320 — ₹1,880
P/E Comps
₹1,380 — ₹1,820
Precedent Txns
₹1,480 — ₹1,960
52-Week Range
₹1,280 — ₹1,920
Analyst Consensus
₹1,520 — ₹1,780
◀ Current price ₹1,590 sits in middle of most ranges → fairly valued. DCF shows wide uncertainty. Precedent txns suggest 10–20% upside on M&A basis.
Football Field — Rules & Best Practices
Ordering the Bars
Precedent Transactions Highest — includes control premium (20–40% over trading price)
DCF Wide range — most variable; reflects assumption uncertainty
Trading Comps Market-anchored; tightest range for liquid, well-covered stocks
52-Week Range Historical context, not intrinsic — show for reference only
LBO Floor Minimum price a PE buyer would pay — useful in M&A context
What Wide vs Narrow Ranges Mean
Wide DCF range High sensitivity to assumptions — model dependent on TV; be humble about single price target
Narrow comps range Liquid stock, many good peers, stable multiples — market pricing is reliable
Gap between DCF & comps DCF above comps → market undervaluing; DCF below → market pricing in growth your model misses
All ranges above price Strong buy — every methodology implies upside
All ranges below price Strong sell — stock expensive on every metric
Interview Tips
💡
Never give a single price without a rangeAlways say "our DCF implies ₹1,590 in the base case, with a bull/bear range of ₹1,040–₹2,160." A single number without context shows you don't understand valuation uncertainty.
💡
Explain why ranges differIf your DCF is much higher than comps, you must explain why: higher growth assumptions, margin expansion thesis, or comps being temporarily depressed. Never leave the gap unexplained.
Trading Comps (Comparable Company Analysis) values a company by applying multiples from similar publicly traded peers. It is the most commonly used valuation method in day-to-day equity research because it is market-anchored, fast to update, and easy to communicate.
How to Build a Trading Comps Table
1
Select 5–8 comparable companies
Screen for: same sector, similar business model, similar size (market cap within 0.5–3×), similar geographies and margins. Avoid including outliers unless you explain why. Quality > quantity.
2
Calculate Enterprise Value for each peer
EV = Market Cap + Total Debt − Cash + Minority Interest
Use current market cap (live price × diluted shares). Use most recent balance sheet for debt and cash.

Treasury Stock Method (TSM): When calculating diluted shares, add in-the-money options/warrants and assume proceeds are used to buy back shares at the current price.
3
Collect consensus estimates (NTM or LTM)
NTM (Next twelve months) multiples are forward-looking and preferred for valuation. LTM (Last twelve months) = historical. Use NTM for growth companies; LTM only for very stable businesses. Source: Bloomberg, NSE/BSE, broker research.
4
Calculate multiples for each peer
EV/EBITDA = EV ÷ NTM EBITDA   P/E = Price ÷ NTM EPS   EV/Revenue = EV ÷ NTM Revenue
5
Calculate median and apply to your target
Use median (not mean — avoids outlier distortion). Apply median multiple to your NTM estimate for the target company. Implied price = (Median EV/EBITDA × NTM EBITDA − Net Debt) ÷ Diluted shares.
6
Apply premium/discount to median if justified
If your company has higher ROCE, faster growth, or stronger brand vs peers → premium. If smaller, lower margins, governance concerns → discount. Always quantify and justify: "we apply a 15% premium to peer median given superior ROCE of 28% vs 18% for peers."
Multiple Selection — Match Numerator & Denominator
Multiple Numerator Denominator Use When
EV / EBITDA Enterprise Value EBITDA Standard for most non-financial companies; capital structure neutral
EV / EBIT Enterprise Value EBIT When D&A is a real economic cost (capex-heavy: power, telecom)
EV / Revenue Enterprise Value Revenue Pre-profit companies, high-growth SaaS/internet, revenue-stage startups
P / E Market Cap Net Income Stable earnings; most intuitive for retail investors; FMCG, IT
P / BV Market Cap Book Equity Banks, NBFCs, insurance — where equity value ≈ book value
P / FCF Market Cap Free Cash Flow Cash generative businesses; cross-check vs P/E for earnings quality
Common Comps Mistakes
⚠
Using LTM multiples for a recovering businessLTM EBITDA may be abnormally depressed (post-COVID, mid-cycle). Apply NTM multiples to forward estimates. Otherwise your "cheap" multiple is just a distressed trailing number.
⚠
Not adjusting for Ind AS 116Post Ind AS 116, EBITDA includes lease add-backs. If some peers adopted it and some haven't (cross-border comps), your EV/EBITDA multiples are not comparable. Always note and adjust.
💡
Always show the growth-adjusted multiple (PEG)A company at 50x P/E growing 30% p.a. is cheaper than one at 35x P/E growing 10%. Show PEG = P/E ÷ growth rate alongside raw multiples to make a proper value argument.
Precedent Transactions (M&A Comps) values a company based on past M&A deals in the exact same sector. Unlike Trading Comps, these multiples include a Control Premium (typically 20–40%) because the acquirer bought control of the business.
How to Build a Precedents Table
1
Screen for relevant M&A transactions
Look for deals in the last 2-3 years, same sector, similar size, and similar geography. Older deals reflect different market environments and may not be relevant.
2
Calculate Transaction Value (EV)
Transaction Value = Offer Price × Fully Diluted Shares + Target Net Debt
Ensure you are calculating the implied enterprise value of the target at the time of the announcement.
3
Find historical financial metrics (LTM)
Unlike Trading Comps (which use NTM), Precedent Transactions overwhelmingly rely on Last Twelve Months (LTM) EBITDA and Revenue as of the deal announcement date. Forward numbers for targets are rarely public at announcement.
4
Calculate transaction multiples
EV / LTM EBITDA   EV / LTM Revenue
Average the multiples across the peer set. Keep in mind: financial buyers (PE firms) generally pay lower multiples than strategic buyers (corporates) who can extract synergies.
Control Premium vs. Synergy
Concept Meaning
Control Premium An acquirer pays a premium over the current share price (usually 20-40%) to incentivize existing shareholders to sell their voting control.
Synergies Cost savings (firing redundant staff) or revenue cross-selling that the buyer expects to realize. This justifies paying the control premium.
Strategic vs PE Strategics pay more because they realize synergies. PE firms (financial sponsors) typically pay less because they lack operational synergies.
Precedent Transaction Pitfalls
⚠
Market Conditions Change: A deal from 2021 (zero interest rate environment) is not comparable to a deal in 2024. Multiples compress dramatically when the cost of capital rises.
⚠
Comparing minority stakes to buyouts: A 10% stake purchase does not carry a control premium and should not be blended with 100% buyouts.
These shortcuts separate analysts who look professional from those who look junior. Interviewers notice. Memorise the top 20 before any technical interview. Updated with the full shortcut library used in IB/valuation workflows.
⚡ Basic Operations
CtrlC
Copy
CtrlV
Paste
CtrlAltV
Paste Special dialog
AltESV
Paste Values Only — removes formulas
CtrlZ
Undo
CtrlY
Redo
CtrlF
Find & Replace — fix assumptions across model
F12
Save As
🧭 Navigation & Selection
Moving Around
Ctrl→
Jump to last cell in data block
CtrlHome
Go to cell A1
CtrlEnd
Go to last used cell in sheet
CtrlG
Go To / Go To Special (jump to named range, blanks, errors)
Page Up
Move up one screen
AltPgDn
Move right one section
Selecting
CtrlShift→
Select to end of row — fast range selection
CtrlShiftEnd
Select from current cell to last used cell
CtrlSpace
Select entire column
ShiftSpace
Select entire row
CtrlA
Select all (press twice for entire sheet)
✏️ Data Editing & Formulas
Editing
F2
Edit cell — see colour-coded precedents
Enter
Confirm and move down
CtrlEnter
Confirm and stay in cell
Esc
Cancel edit
CtrlD
Fill Down — extend formula down a column fast
CtrlR
Fill Right — copy formula across columns
Ctrl;
Insert today's date (hardcoded)
Formula Shortcuts
F4
Repeat last action OR toggle absolute reference ($)
Alt=
Quick SUM — auto-sums selected range
Ctrl`
Show all formulas — spot hardcodes instantly
Clearing
Delete
Clear cell data only
AltHEF
Clear cell format only
AltHEA
Clear all (data + format)
🎨 Formatting
Number Formats
Ctrl1
Format Cells dialog
CtrlShift1
Number format (2 decimal places)
CtrlShift2
Percent format
CtrlShift3
Date format
CtrlShift$
Currency format
AltH0
Increase decimal places
AltH9

Font & Style
CtrlB
Bold
CtrlI
Italic
CtrlU
Underline
AltHH
Fill colour — colour-code assumptions vs formulas
AltHFC
Change font colour
AltHFS
Change font size
Alignment
AltHAC

AltHAL

AltHAR
Align right
AltH6
Increase indent
AltH5
Decrease indent
ShiftF2

AltHEM

📊 Rows, Columns & Groups
CtrlShift+
Insert row / column
Ctrl-
Delete row / column
AltHOI
AutoFit column width
AltHOW

AltHOH

AltShift→

AltShift←

AltAJ

🔍 Auditing & Formula Tools
Ctrl[
Trace Precedents — where does this cell pull from?
Ctrl]
Trace Dependents — which cells use this?
AltMP
Trace Precedents (ribbon path)
F9
Evaluate selected formula part (debug)
AltHSF
Filter — apply filter to data range
CtrlShiftL
Toggle AutoFilter — filter tables instantly
AltHSC

📑 Worksheet Navigation
CtrlPgDn

CtrlPgUp

ShiftF11
New worksheet
AltHDS

AltHOR

AltHOM

AltWFF

AltWFR
