🏆 Top 10 Every Analyst Must Know Cold
AltESV
Paste Values Only — removes formulas, pastes numbers
F4
Lock reference ($) — toggle $A$1 / A$1 / $A1 / A1
CtrlD
Fill Down — extend formula down a column fast
Ctrl`
Show all formulas — spot hardcodes instantly
Ctrl[
Trace Precedents — audit where cells pull from
Alt=
Quick SUM — auto-sums a column or row instantly
CtrlShiftL
Toggle AutoFilter — filter tables instantly
AltHH
Fill colour — colour-code assumptions vs formulas
Ctrl1
Format Cells dialog — full control over any format
F2
Enter edit mode — see colour-coded precedents
📐 Excel Functions Reference
The 50 functions used in IB, ER, and valuation models — organised by category with syntax and purpose. The top 15 most-used in finance models are: SUM · IF · INDEX · MATCH · XLOOKUP · SUMIFS · COUNTIFS · IFERROR · IRR · XIRR · NPV · EOMONTH · OFFSET · LEFT/RIGHT · LEN
🔎 Lookup & Reference
Function Syntax Purpose
XLOOKUP =XLOOKUP(lookup_value, lookup_array, return_array, [if_not_found]) Modern replacement for VLOOKUP — searches any direction, returns full row/column
VLOOKUP =VLOOKUP(lookup_value, table_array, col_index_num, [range_lookup]) Vertical lookup — finds value in first column, returns value from specified column
HLOOKUP =HLOOKUP(lookup_value, table_array, row_index_num, [range_lookup]) Horizontal lookup — same as VLOOKUP but scans rows instead of columns
INDEX =INDEX(array, row_num, [column_num]) Returns value at intersection of row & column — used with MATCH for flexible lookups
MATCH =MATCH(lookup_value, lookup_array, [match_type]) Returns position of value in a range — pairs with INDEX to replace VLOOKUP
OFFSET =OFFSET(reference, rows, cols, [height], [width]) Returns a range offset from a starting cell — used for dynamic ranges in models
CHOOSE =CHOOSE(index_num, value1, [value2], …) Returns value from a list by position — used for scenario toggles (Base/Bull/Bear)
INDIRECT =INDIRECT(ref_text, [a1]) Returns reference from a text string — used for dynamic sheet references
ADDRESS =ADDRESS(row_num, column_num, [abs_num]) Returns a cell address as text from row/column numbers
ROW =ROW([reference]) Returns the row number of a reference — used for dynamic numbering
➕ Math & Statistical
Function Syntax Purpose
SUM =SUM(number1, [number2], …) Adds numbers — most-used function in any model
SUMIF =SUMIF(range, criteria, [sum_range]) Sums values that meet one condition
SUMIFS =SUMIFS(sum_range, criteria_range1, criteria1, …) Sums values meeting multiple conditions — e.g. sum revenue for FY2025 + specific segment
AVERAGE =AVERAGE(number1, [number2], …) Calculates arithmetic mean of a range
AVERAGEIF =AVERAGEIF(range, criteria, [average_range]) Average of values meeting one condition
COUNT =COUNT(value1, [value2], …) Counts cells containing numbers
COUNTA =COUNTA(value1, [value2], …) Counts non-empty cells (numbers + text)
COUNTIF =COUNTIF(range, criteria) Counts cells meeting one condition
COUNTIFS =COUNTIFS(criteria_range1, criteria1, …) Counts cells meeting multiple conditions
SUBTOTAL =SUBTOTAL(function_num, ref1, [ref2], …) Applies function (SUM/AVG etc.) ignoring hidden/filtered rows — use 9 for SUM
💰 Financial Functions — Core for DCF & Valuation
Function Syntax Purpose
NPV =NPV(rate, value1, [value2], …) Net present value of cash flows at a discount rate — does not include Year 0 outflow
XNPV =XNPV(rate, values, dates) NPV with irregular cash flow dates — more accurate than NPV for real models
IRR =IRR(values, [guess]) Internal rate of return — assumes equal time periods between cash flows
XIRR =XIRR(values, dates, [guess]) IRR with irregular dates — use this for PE/VC return calculations
PV =PV(rate, nper, pmt, [fv], [type]) Present value of a series of payments at a constant rate
FV =FV(rate, nper, pmt, [pv], [type]) Future value of a series of payments
PMT =PMT(rate, nper, pv, [fv], [type]) Periodic payment for a loan — used in debt schedule modelling
RATE =RATE(nper, pmt, pv, [fv], [type], [guess]) Interest rate per period — solve for yield from payment stream
NPER =NPER(rate, pmt, pv, [fv], [type]) Number of periods for a loan/investment to reach target
MIRR =MIRR(values, finance_rate, reinvest_rate) Modified IRR — addresses reinvestment rate assumption flaw in standard IRR
⚖️ Logical & Error Handling
Function Syntax Purpose
IF =IF(logical_test, value_if_true, value_if_false) Returns one value if condition is true, another if false — backbone of model logic
IFS =IFS(condition1, value1, condition2, value2, …) Multiple IF conditions without nesting — cleaner than nested IFs
AND =AND(logical1, logical2, …) Returns TRUE if all conditions are true
OR =OR(logical1, logical2, …) Returns TRUE if any condition is true
NOT =NOT(logical) Reverses a logical value — TRUE becomes FALSE
IFERROR =IFERROR(value, value_if_error) Returns alternate value if formula errors — wraps VLOOKUP/MATCH to suppress #N/A
IFNA =IFNA(value, value_if_na) Handles only #N/A errors — more precise than IFERROR
SWITCH =SWITCH(expression, value1, result1, …) Matches expression to a list of values and returns result — cleaner than nested IFs
🔤 Text Functions
Function Syntax Purpose
LEFT =LEFT(text, [num_chars]) Extracts characters from the left — used to parse ticker codes, dates as text
RIGHT =RIGHT(text, [num_chars]) Extracts characters from the right
MID =MID(text, start_num, num_chars) Extracts characters from the middle of a string
LEN =LEN(text) Returns length of a string — used with LEFT/RIGHT/MID for dynamic parsing
TRIM =TRIM(text) Removes extra spaces — critical for cleaning Bloomberg/Capitaline data imports
UPPER =UPPER(text) Converts text to uppercase
LOWER =LOWER(text) Converts text to lowercase
PROPER =PROPER(text) Capitalises first letter of each word
CONCAT =CONCAT(text1, text2, …) Joins text strings — use for dynamic labels in charts and dashboards
TEXT =TEXT(value, format_text) Converts number to formatted text — e.g. =TEXT(A1,"₹#,##0") for display
📅 Date & Time Functions
Function Syntax Purpose
TODAY =TODAY() Returns today's date — live, updates daily
NOW =NOW() Returns current date and time
YEAR =YEAR(serial_number) Extracts year from a date — used to map cash flows to fiscal year columns
MONTH =MONTH(serial_number) Extracts month number from a date
DAY =DAY(serial_number) Extracts day number from a date
EOMONTH =EOMONTH(start_date, months) Last day of month N months away — essential for period-end date calculations
DATEDIF =DATEDIF(start_date, end_date, unit) Difference between two dates in days/months/years — use "Y", "M", or "D"
WORKDAY =WORKDAY(start_date, days, [holidays]) Returns date N working days from start — excludes weekends & holidays
NETWORKDAYS =NETWORKDAYS(start_date, end_date, [holidays]) Counts working days between two dates
EDATE =EDATE(start_date, months) Date N months from start date — used for rolling period calculations
The 3-statement model (Income Statement, Balance Sheet, Cash Flow Statement) is the foundation of all financial modelling. Every LBO, DCF, and M&A model is built on top of it. You must be able to explain how all three link together in any interview.
🔗 How the Three Statements Link
The Complete Linkage Map
Income Statement
Revenue → Net Income
→
Net Income flows into Retained Earnings on Balance Sheet
AND into Cash Flow Statement (top of CFO)
Cash Flow Statement
CFO + CFI + CFF
→
Ending Cash = Cash on Balance Sheet
D&A added back in CFO (non-cash from IS)
Capex flows to PP&E on Balance Sheet
Balance Sheet
Assets = Liabilities + Equity
→
Working Capital changes flow into CFO
Debt draws/repayments flow into CFF
Must balance every period — if not, model has an error
📋 Income Statement — Key Lines
Line Item What to Know
Revenue Top line. Model by segment/product/geography. Use volume × price drivers.
COGS Direct costs. Gross margin = (Revenue − COGS) / Revenue. Watch for mix shifts.
Gross Profit Revenue − COGS. Key profitability signal at product level.
EBITDA Gross Profit − Opex (excl. D&A). Operating cash proxy. Most-used valuation metric.
D&A Non-cash. Reduces EBIT but added back in CFS. Drives deferred tax.
EBIT EBITDA − D&A. Also = Operating Income. Used in EBIT × (1−t) for NOPAT.
Interest Driven by debt schedule. Creates circularity if debt depends on cash balance.
EBT EBIT − Net Interest. Apply effective tax rate to get net income.
Net Income Feeds retained earnings (BS) and top of cash flow statement.
💵 Cash Flow Statement — Structure
1
CFO — Cash from Operations
Start with Net Income. Add back D&A (non-cash). Adjust for Working Capital changes (↑AR = cash outflow, ↑AP = cash inflow). Also add back other non-cash items (stock comp, provisions).
2
CFI — Cash from Investing
Capex (negative — cash out). Asset disposals (positive). Acquisitions (negative). Changes in investments. Capex links to PP&E on balance sheet.
3
CFF — Cash from Financing
Debt raised (positive) / repaid (negative). Equity issuance / buybacks. Dividends paid. Links directly to debt schedule and equity section of BS.
4
Ending Cash = Beginning Cash + CFO + CFI + CFF
This ending cash balance must equal the Cash line on the Balance Sheet. This is the key check that your model balances.
⚖️ Balance Sheet Mechanics
Key Balance Sheet Links
BS Line Driven By
Cash Ending cash from CFS (the plug)
Accounts Receivable Revenue × (DSO / 365)
Inventory COGS × (DIO / 365)
PP&E (net) Prior PP&E + Capex − D&A
Accounts Payable COGS × (DPO / 365)
Debt Prior debt + draws − repayments (debt schedule)
Retained Earnings Prior RE + Net Income − Dividends
Working Capital — The Formula
NWC = Current Assets − Current Liabilities = (AR + Inventory + Prepaid) − (AP + Accrued Liabilities) Change in NWC (for CFS): ↑ NWC = Cash Outflow (negative in CFO) ↓ NWC = Cash Inflow (positive in CFO) DSO = AR / Revenue × 365 DIO = Inventory / COGS × 365 DPO = AP / COGS × 365 CCC = DSO + DIO − DPO
💡
Interview tip: If a company's DSO is rising quarter on quarter, it means customers are paying more slowly — a red flag for revenue quality. Interviewers love this insight.
The debt schedule is the most complex part of a 3-statement model. It tracks every tranche of debt, calculates interest expense (which feeds the Income Statement), and models repayments (which feed the Cash Flow Statement). Getting this right is what separates good models from great ones.
🏗️ How to Build a Debt Schedule
Step-by-Step Build
1
List every debt tranche separately
Revolving Credit Facility (RCF), Term Loan A, Term Loan B, Senior Notes, Subordinated Debt. Each has its own interest rate, maturity, and repayment terms.
2
Opening Balance = Prior Year Closing Balance
For Year 1: use transaction debt amounts. Each subsequent year rolls forward. Opening → + Draws − Mandatory Repayments − Optional Prepayments = Closing.
3
Calculate Interest on Average Balance
Interest = (Opening + Closing) / 2 × Interest Rate. Using average balance is more accurate than opening or closing alone. This creates a circular reference (see Circularity tab).
4
Model mandatory vs optional repayments
Mandatory: amortisation schedule (e.g. 1% per year for TLB). Optional: cash sweep — excess cash after mandatory payments used to repay debt. Use MIN(available cash, outstanding debt).
5
Link outputs to IS and CFS
Total interest expense → Income Statement (below EBIT). Total repayments + draws → CFF section of Cash Flow Statement. Closing balance → Balance Sheet debt lines.
Debt Schedule Template
FY25E FY26E FY27E ───────────────────────────────────────── TERM LOAN B (₹ Cr) Opening Balance 1,000 950 900 + Draws — — — − Mandatory Repay (50) (50) (50) − Cash Sweep — — (100) Closing Balance 950 900 750 Interest Rate 9.5% 9.5% 9.5% Avg Balance 975 925 825 Interest Expense 92.6 87.9 78.4 ───────────────────────────────────────── REVOLVING CREDIT (₹ Cr) Opening Balance — 200 — + Draws 200 — — − Repayments — (200) — Closing Balance 200 — — Interest Rate 10.5% 10.5% — Interest Expense 21.0 — — ───────────────────────────────────────── TOTAL INTEREST → Income Statement 113.6 87.9 78.4
💡 Key Debt Concepts for Interviews
Term Meaning
PIK Interest Payment-in-kind — interest added to principal instead of paid in cash. Common in distressed/PE deals. No cash outflow but debt grows.
Cash Sweep Mandatory use of excess cash to repay debt. Lender protection. Models as MIN(excess cash, outstanding debt).
Covenant Financial ratio limit (e.g. Net Debt/EBITDA < 4.0x). Breach = event of default. Model a covenant tracker alongside debt schedule.
Amortisation Scheduled principal repayment. TLA typically amortises faster than TLB. Bullet = all principal at maturity.
DSCR Debt Service Coverage = EBITDA / (Interest + Principal). Lenders want >1.2x. Below 1.0x = cannot service debt from operations.
Net Debt Total Debt − Cash. Used in bridge from EV to Equity Value. Always use gross debt for interest, net debt for valuation.
🏦 Typical LBO Debt Structure
Tranche % of Capital Rate Maturity
RCF (undrawn) 10–15% of TLA MIBOR+300 5 yr
Term Loan A 20–25% MIBOR+350 5 yr
Term Loan B 30–40% MIBOR+450 7 yr
Senior Notes 15–20% 10–12% 8 yr
Sub Debt / Mezz 5–10% 14–18% 9 yr
Equity (PE) 30–40% — —
💡
Interview tip: In Indian PE, typical leverage is 3–4x Net Debt/EBITDA vs 5–6x in US LBOs. Lenders are more conservative.
Circular references are one of the most feared topics in modelling interviews. They are common, unavoidable in realistic models, and must be handled correctly. If you say "I just avoid circularity" in an interview — that's a red flag.
🔄 What Causes Circularity?
The Classic Circularity Chain
Why interest expense creates a circle:
Interest Expense (IS) → reduces Net Income → reduces Retained Earnings → affects Cash Balance → affects Debt Repayment (cash sweep) → changes Debt Balance → changes Interest Expense ↩
Other common circular loops:
• Revolver draws: Cash shortfall → draw on revolver → revolver interest → reduces cash → bigger shortfall

• Tax on interest: Interest reduces EBT → changes tax → changes net income → changes cash → changes debt → changes interest

• Dividend policy: Dividend = % of net income → net income includes interest → interest depends on cash → cash depends on dividend
How to Handle Circularity — 3 Methods
1
Enable Iterative Calculation Preferred
File → Options → Formulas → Enable Iterative Calculation. Set max iterations to 100, max change to 0.001. Excel will recalculate the circle until it converges. Clean, no workarounds needed.
2
Circular Reference Switch Common in IB
Add a toggle cell (0 or 1). When 0: use prior year interest (breaks the circle). When 1: use current year interest (model runs live). Formula: =IF(switch=0, prior_interest, current_interest). Prevents #REF errors when model is first built.
3
Use Beginning Balance for Interest Simple
Instead of average balance, calculate interest on opening balance only. Slightly less accurate but eliminates the circularity entirely. Acceptable for quick models or early-stage work.
⚠️
Never leave a circular reference unresolved. Without iterative calc enabled, Excel will show zeros or #REF errors, making your entire model wrong. Always check this setting when opening a new file.
Interview Script — "How do you handle circular references?"
"Circular references arise naturally in financial models — the most common example is interest expense, which depends on the debt balance, which depends on cash available for repayment, which depends on net income, which depends on interest expense. I handle this by enabling iterative calculation in Excel's settings, which allows the model to converge on a solution over multiple passes. In more complex models or when sharing with people who may not have iterative calc enabled, I use a circularity switch — a toggle cell that breaks the loop when set to zero, preventing #REF errors, and re-enables the live calculation when set to one. The key is to always be aware of where the circles exist in your model so you can explain and defend them."
These are the most common errors that interviewers look for — and that cause models to go wrong in practice. Know them cold. Being able to spot and name mistakes in someone else's model is a highly valued skill.
❌ The Most Costly Modelling Mistakes
⚠️
Hardcoding numbers inside formulas — Writing =Revenue*0.35 instead of =Revenue*GrossMarginAssumption. When assumptions change, hardcoded values don't update. Every assumption must live in a clearly labelled input cell.
⚠️
Balance sheet that doesn't balance — Assets ≠ Liabilities + Equity. Most common cause: forgetting to link Net Income to Retained Earnings, or missing a working capital item. Always build a balance check cell (=Assets−Liabilities−Equity should = 0).
⚠️
Mixing time periods — Using annual depreciation in a monthly model without dividing by 12. Using period-end balances for interest instead of averages. Fiscal year vs calendar year confusion for Indian companies (FY = April–March).
⚠️
Unresolved circular references — Leaving circles without iterative calculation enabled, causing the model to return 0 or #REF everywhere. Always check: File → Options → Formulas → Iterative Calculation.
⚠️
Confusing EV and Equity Value — Using EV multiples to value equity directly, or forgetting to add cash and subtract debt in the bridge. EV = Equity Value + Net Debt + Minorities + Preferred − Associates.
⚠️
Wrong sign conventions — Capex shown as positive when it should be negative (cash outflow). Debt repayment shown as positive. Inconsistent sign convention across CFS makes the model wrong even if individual lines are right.
⚠️
Double-counting D&A — D&A reduces EBIT on the IS, then gets added back on the CFS. Some modellers accidentally subtract it twice, or forget to add it back. Always trace D&A through all three statements.
⚠️
Terminal value that dominates — If TV is more than 85–90% of total EV, your explicit forecast period is too short or your near-term FCFs are too low. Extend the period or re-examine assumptions. A TV-heavy model is not a model — it's a guess.
⚠️
Using net income instead of FCF for DCF — Net income is an accounting number. DCF requires free cash flow = NOPAT + D&A − Capex − Change in NWC. Using net income ignores capex intensity and working capital needs.
⚠️
Not stress-testing assumptions — Presenting a single output without a sensitivity table. Every model output is a range, not a point. Failing to show sensitivities signals inexperience. Always build a WACC × growth rate table minimum.
Professional models follow a strict set of conventions. These make models auditable, shareable, and error-resistant. Following these signals you've worked in a real finance environment.
🎨 Colour Coding Convention (Industry Standard)
Colour Meaning Example
■ Blue text Hard-coded assumption / input Revenue growth rate: 12%
■ Black text Formula / calculated cell =Revenue*(1+GrowthRate)
■ Green text Link from another sheet/file ='Actuals'!B5
■ Red text Error check / warning cell =IF(BSCheck<>0,"ERROR","")
■ Grey shading Historical / actual data FY22, FY23, FY24 columns
■ Yellow fill Input/assumption cell WACC input, growth rate input
📐 Model Structure Rules
1
One input, used everywhere
Each assumption lives in exactly one cell. All other cells reference that cell. Never type the same number twice in a model.
2
Inputs on one sheet, outputs on others
Separate "Assumptions" tab from "IS", "BS", "CFS" tabs. Auditor or reviewer can change all inputs in one place.
3
Formulas consistent across a row
Every cell in a forecast row should have the same formula — just copy right. If one cell has a different formula, it will be wrong and hard to find.
4
Error checks on every output sheet
BS check (=0), CFS check (ending cash = BS cash), IS check (retained earnings roll). Display prominently. Model is not done until all checks are zero.
✅ Pre-Submission Checklist
□
Balance sheet balances every year (Assets = L + E)
□
Ending cash in CFS = Cash on balance sheet
□
No #REF, #DIV/0!, #VALUE errors anywhere
□
Iterative calculation enabled (if model has circularity)
□
All assumptions are blue / in dedicated input cells
□
Sensitivity table built (WACC × g minimum)
□
Historical data matches company filings
□
Units consistent (₹ Cr vs ₹ Mn — label clearly)
□
No hardcoded numbers inside formulas
□
Margins and ratios look reasonable vs peers
🗣️ Interview Script — "Walk me through a 3-statement model"
"I start with the Income Statement, projecting revenue using volume and price drivers, then building down to EBITDA, EBIT, and Net Income by modelling margins and D&A. Net Income flows into two places: the top of the Cash Flow Statement and into Retained Earnings on the Balance Sheet.

The Cash Flow Statement starts with net income, adds back non-cash items like D&A, adjusts for working capital changes, then shows Capex in the investing section and debt movements in financing. The ending cash balance must equal the cash line on the Balance Sheet — that's my primary model check.

The Balance Sheet links everything together: PP&E rolls with Capex and D&A, working capital lines are driven by DSO/DIO/DPO assumptions, debt is driven by the debt schedule, and equity builds through retained earnings. If the balance sheet doesn't balance, there's an error somewhere in those linkages."

💬 Interview Answer Templates auto-fills from your latest data above
Q: "What is your macro view on India right now?"
"India is in a monetary easing cycle. RBI cut repo to 5.25% in Feb 2025 — more cuts expected as CPI moderates to 3.21%, well within the 4±2% band. GDP at 6.5% for FY25 — a moderation from FY24 but still the fastest among major economies. FII flows are negative CY25 due to USD strength, but DII and SIP flows are absorbing selling. INR at ₹92.7 is under pressure but manageable — RBI has $688 bn in reserves, about 11 months of import cover."
Q: "Which sectors benefit from the current macro?"
"Rate cuts benefit banks & NBFCs (lower cost of funds + credit demand pickup), real estate (lower EMIs → affordability), and auto (financing cost falls). Weak INR at ₹92.7 is a tailwind for IT services and pharma exporters — USD revenue converts to more rupees. Low crude at $109 helps airlines, paints, and OMCs. GST at ₹2.0L Cr signals consumption is holding — positive for FMCG and consumer discretionary."
RBI Rate Cycle — Where Are We?
Peak (6.50%)
May 2022 – Feb 2023
Hold
Feb 2023 – Feb 2025
▼ Cutting
Feb 2025 onwards
RBI cut 25bps in Feb 2025 — the first cut since May 2020. Market pricing 50–75bps total cuts in FY26 as CPI converges toward the 4% target. Rate-sensitive sectors (banking, real estate, NBFCs) are the primary beneficiaries.
Key dates to track
MPC meetings: Apr, Jun, Aug, Oct, Dec 2025
CPI release: 2nd week each month (MoSPI)
GDP advance estimate: January each year
Macro → Sector Impact (Current Reading)
Macro Factor Reading Sectors Helped ↑ Sectors Hurt ↓
Repo Rate 5.25% Cutting Banks, RE, Auto, NBFCs —
CPI 3.21% within band FMCG, Bonds —
INR/USD ₹92.7 weak IT, Pharma, Metals Airlines, Paints

FII Flows -₹1.6L Cr FY26 DII-heavy midcaps Nifty large caps

💬 More Interview Answer Templates
Q: "What is the state of India's fiscal policy?"
"India is on a fiscal consolidation path — the deficit narrows from 5.1% in FY24 to a target of 4.4% in FY26. The key feature of the current budget philosophy is capex-led growth — ₹11.1L Cr in FY26 infrastructure spend is designed to crowd-in private investment. Revenue capex (roads, railways, urban infra) multipliers are estimated at 2.5–3x vs 1x for revenue expenditure. However, divestment has historically underdelivered, and interest payments now consume ~20% of government revenues — a structural constraint on fiscal flexibility."
Q: "How healthy is India's banking system?"
"India's banks are in the best shape in a decade. Gross NPAs have fallen to ~2.6% — a 12-year low — and capital adequacy (CRAR at 16.8%) is well above the regulatory minimum. The prior NPA crisis of 2015–2019 has been substantially resolved through IBC, bank recapitalisations, and write-offs. The current risk to watch is the elevated credit-deposit ratio (~78%) — credit has grown faster than deposits, which RBI has flagged as a systemic liquidity risk. Unsecured retail lending growth is also being monitored after RBI raised risk weights in late 2023."
Q: "What are the key risks to India's growth story?"
"I'd highlight four: (1) Geopolitical & crude oil risk — India imports ~85% of oil; a supply shock to Brent significantly widens the CAD and INR. (2) INR depreciation — at ₹86.5/$, further weakening raises import costs and triggers FII outflows. (3) Private capex gap — government has been doing the heavy lifting; private sector investment has been sluggish due to capacity utilisation concerns. (4) Rural consumption lag — despite urban recovery, rural demand remains uneven, making inflation in food (50% of CPI basket) a political and economic risk."
Q: "How do global factors affect Indian equities?"
"Three global factors dominate Indian equity flows: (1) US Fed policy — when the Fed holds rates high, the DXY strengthens (currently ~108), making EM assets less attractive and driving FII outflows from India (net -₹90,000 Cr in CY25 YTD). (2) Crude oil — Brent at $74 is benign; above $90–95 it creates a stagflation risk for India via higher inflation and a wider CAD. (3) US–China tensions — paradoxically a tailwind for India via supply chain diversification and China+1 manufacturing FDI."
Q: "Explain India's Current Account and what defends the INR?"
"India structurally runs a current account deficit — goods imports (oil, electronics, gold) exceed exports, creating a merchandise deficit of ~$23bn/month. This is partially offset by a services surplus (~$160bn annually from IT/BPO/travel) and remittances (~$125bn, world's largest). The CAD is currently manageable at ~1.2% of GDP. The INR is defended by RBI's $625bn forex reserves (~11 months import cover) and active intervention — the RBI sold dollars aggressively when INR breached 87 to prevent overshooting. The risk is a sharp crude spike widening the CAD and forcing the RBI to choose between reserves and INR."
Q: "What does the SIP/MF data tell you about Indian markets?"
"SIP inflows at ₹26,400 Cr/month (record) and MF AUM of ₹67L Cr are structural, not cyclical — they reflect the financialisation of household savings, with 10 Cr+ SIP accounts. This has two market implications: (1) DII buying power absorbs FII selling (which is exactly what happened in CY25 when FIIs sold ₹90,000 Cr but markets didn't collapse). (2) Valuation floor — consistent domestic demand has compressed the typical correction depth. Historically Nifty corrections were 25–30%; the CY25 correction to ~22,500 is only ~15%. The risk: if SIPs slow during a deep correction, the support mechanism weakens — a 'SIP shock absorber' hypothesis that hasn't been stress-tested in a prolonged bear market."
⚡ Quick Cheat Sheet — Numbers to Know Cold
India GDP size: ~$3.7 trillion (FY25E) — 5th largest globally
Nominal GDP growth: ~10.5% (real 6.4% + ~4% deflator)
Per capita GDP: ~$2,600 (USD) — still EM, huge headroom
RBI inflation target: 4% ± 2% band (2–6% tolerance)
Food weight in CPI: ~46% — why monsoon drives inflation
India's oil import bill: ~$130–150bn/yr (~85% imported)
Nifty 50 P/E (current): ~19x — below 5-yr avg of ~22x
India market cap/GDP: ~130% — elevated but justified by growth
NSE listed companies: 2,200+ (market cap ~₹380L Cr)
IBC resolution rate: ~45% recovery vs 25% in old BIFR regime
PLI scheme outlay: ₹1.97L Cr across 14 sectors
India FDI inflows FY25: ~$45bn — manufacturing & services

Describe different methods used to analyze financial statements.
Equity Researchunseen
Answer
Answer: Ratio Analysis: Evaluate liquidity (current, quick), solvency (debt/equity, interest coverage), profitability (ROA, ROE, gross/net margin) and efficiency (inventory turnover, receivables days) to quantify performance and compare over time or peers. Horizontal (Trend) Analysis: Compare financial line items across periods to detect growth rates, trends, seasonality and unusual movements. Vertical (Common-Size) Analysis: Express each item as a percentage of a base (sales for income statement, total assets for balance sheet) to compare companies of different sizes.
